bridge.title: Not All Thoughts Need HBM: Semantics-Aware Memory Hierarchy for LLM Reasoning
bridge.abstract: Reasoning LLMs produce thousands of chain-of-thought tokens whose KV cache must reside in scarce GPU HBM. The dominant response -- permanently evicting low-importance tokens -- is catastrophic for reasoning: accuracy collapses to 0-2.5% when half the cache is removed. We ask a different question: must every token live in HBM, or can some live elsewhere? We introduce a semantics-aware memory hierarchy that sorts tokens into four tiers -- HBM, DDR, compressed, and evicted -- using cumulative attention scoring. Low-importance tokens are moved to CPU memory rather than destroyed; before each attention step they are prefetched back at full precision, contributing exactly the same terms as if they had never left the GPU. We formalize this as zero-approximation-error offloading and derive our central finding: accuracy depends solely on how many tokens are permanently discarded (the eviction ratio), not on how many remain in HBM. A controlled 3x3 grid over HBM and eviction ratios confirms this across three model scales (7B-32B) and four benchmarks. With only 3% eviction, the hierarchy retains 91% of full-cache accuracy on GSM8K and 71% on MATH-500 (n=200); at 14B scale it matches the uncompressed baseline (90% vs. 86%) while halving HBM occupancy. A head-to-head reproduction of R-KV -- the current SOTA eviction method -- on our setup achieves only 0-32% at comparable budgets. A system prototype with real GPU-CPU data movement shows that the price of this preservation is modest -- 5-7% transfer overhead -- and scaling analysis projects 2-48 GB HBM savings at production batch sizes.
bridge.why_now: Reasoning-heavy inference is making KV cache pressure a live operational problem, and this paper reframes the issue in a useful way: the real damage comes from eviction, not from moving tokens out of HBM. That is timely for both broad AI audiences following reasoning models and practitioners thinking about memory tiering and serving cost.
bridge.why_not_higher: The evidence is strong enough to take seriously, but it is still centered on reasoning decode workloads with relatively limited benchmark sample sizes and a modest system prototype. The production-scale savings claims are partly projected rather than fully demonstrated end to end.
downgrade_reasons: Reasoning-focused workload may limit generality
Small evaluation slices on key benchmarks
Production savings are partly projection-based
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: A larger end-to-end serving study on frontier reasoning models with clear latency, throughput, and cost tradeoffs would raise priority further.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the reason KV-cache pruning destroys reasoning is not that every thought must stay in HBM, but that we keep deleting thoughts that could have been parked elsewhere and fetched back exactly when needed?

bridge.title: Think Before You Grid-Search: Floor-First Triage for LLM Serving
bridge.abstract: LLM serving optimization typically benchmarks many configurations and reaches for heavy profilers when latency targets are missed. We argue for the reverse discipline: estimation is the analytical layer of profiling -- without it, optimization degenerates to grid search. Floor First is a residual-driven triage workflow. Each decode step is modeled as a five-dimensional resource vector (HBM bytes, FLOPs, network bytes, network messages, KV capacity); summing within a resource and maximizing across resources gives an optimistic floor, the plain sum a pessimistic one. Where a measurement lands inside this [max, sum] interval reads out overlap quality before any profiler is opened, and profilers escalate only on residuals above a stated threshold. Deployment alternatives are compared by wall ordering -- which resource wall binds first as load grows -- rather than by point benchmarks. The account is compositional: new attention or state-space variants enter by declaring one module, and the workflow ships as a zero-dependency calculator plus an agent skill that enforces the discipline in agentic optimization loops.   As a case study we analyze a DeepSeek-V3.2-style 671B MoE/MLA model on 16 NVIDIA H20 GPUs, whose ridge point of ~74 FLOP/byte (vs ~590 for H100) makes it an extreme decode-oriented part. The floors show TP16 decoding is KV-capacity-limited to ~70 concurrent 8K requests; sparse attention removes the KV-bandwidth term but not the capacity wall; an EP16+DP-attention layout accepts slightly worse same-batch weight traffic for an order-of-magnitude higher capacity wall (~644) -- while single-stream latency favors TP by 2.4x. The layout judgment is thus a computable function of the operating point, explaining why production deployments on identical hardware have shipped opposite attention layouts.
bridge.why_now: Inference teams are actively choosing between tensor-parallel and expert-parallel attention layouts on constrained accelerator fleets, and this paper offers a simple way to predict the binding wall before burning time on profiler-heavy sweeps. It turns KV capacity, bandwidth, and overlap into a clear operating-point story that is timely and easy to teach.
bridge.why_not_higher: The support is still mainly an analytical framework plus one detailed large-model case study rather than a broad empirical campaign across stacks and workloads. Its appeal is strongest for serving and systems listeners, not the full AI audience.
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Validation across multiple model families, serving stacks, and real production traces would make the method harder to dismiss as a smart but narrow serving heuristic.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the fastest way to fix LLM serving is not another benchmark sweep, but a five-number floor calculation that tells you which wall will fail first?

bridge.title: PRISM: Fast Online LLM Serving via Scheduling-Memory Co-design
bridge.abstract: Modern online large language model (LLM) services, such as Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and agent systems, increasingly expose two prominent characteristics: prompt segmentation (e.g., system instructions, retrieved passages, tool outputs) and hotspot skew, where a small set of these segments recurs frequently across user requests. Failing to jointly exploit these patterns could lead to repeated prefill of hot segments and prolonged TTFT, undermining both throughput and user-perceived responsiveness. However, existing work tackles these patterns independently: KV-cache management mainly exploits segment reuse while scheduling reorders requests to improve cache locality, yet neither aligns request admission with KV-cache retention. To address this gap, we first analyze how scheduling and KV-cache management jointly affect TTFT. Guided by this, we present PRISM (Prefix Reuse Optimization Integrated Scheduling and Memory), which co-designs a query-aware scheduler (QAS) with a demand-aware radix tree (DART) to align request admission with exact-prefix KV retention. Our evaluation results show that, versus the strongest baseline, PRISM reduces average per-QPS P99 TTFT by 23.3\% and 37.1\% while increasing exact-prefix KV-cache hit rate by 5.9 and 12.2 percentage points on 4B and 13B models, respectively.
bridge.why_now: RAG and agent serving stacks make segmented prompts and reusable prefixes a live systems problem, so TTFT gains tied to exact-prefix KV reuse matter now. PRISM is timely because it connects two knobs operators already touch separately: scheduling and KV-cache retention.
bridge.why_not_higher: The result looks strong, but the abstract-level evidence is still bounded to 4B and 13B evaluations and does not yet prove robustness across larger deployments or varied hardware. It is a sharp serving advance, but not a major conceptual shift for the whole AI field.
downgrade_reasons: Audience pull is stronger for inference and systems builders than for the full AI research audience
Evidence shown here is limited to abstract-level results on 4B and 13B models
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same gains on larger production-class models with clearer ablations across hardware and mixed RAG or agent traffic patterns.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: PRISM asks a useful serving question: if hot prompt segments dominate LLM traffic, why are the scheduler and the KV cache still making decisions independently?

bridge.title: TensorHub: Scalable and Elastic Weight Transfer for LLM RL Training
bridge.abstract: Modern LLM reinforcement learning (RL) workloads require a highly efficient weight transfer system to scale training across heterogeneous computational resources. However, existing weight transfer approaches either fail to provide flexibility for dynamically scaling clusters or incur fundamental data movement overhead, resulting in poor performance.   We introduce Reference-Oriented Storage (ROS), a new storage abstraction for RL weight transfer that exploits the highly replicated model weights in place. ROS presents the illusion that certain versions of the model weights are stored and can be fetched on demand. Underneath, ROS does not physically store any copies of the weights; instead, it tracks the workers that hold these weights on GPUs for inference. Upon request, ROS directly uses them to serve reads. We build TensorHub, a production-quality system that extends the ROS idea with topology-optimized transfer, strong consistency, and fault tolerance. Evaluation shows that TensorHub fully saturates RDMA bandwidth and adapts to three distinct rollout workloads with minimal engineering effort. Specifically, TensorHub reduces total GPU stall time by up to 6.7x for standalone rollouts, accelerates weight update for elastic rollout by 4.8x, and cuts cross-datacenter rollout stall time by 19x. TensorHub has been deployed in production to support cutting-edge RL training.
bridge.why_now: Post-training RL is moving from a niche alignment workflow toward mainstream LLM infrastructure, and this paper targets a real scaling bottleneck: moving weights between trainers and rollout workers. The core idea is timely, concrete, and supported by production-style systems evidence rather than toy benchmarks.
bridge.why_not_higher: The scope is still fairly specific to large RL rollout pipelines with replicated weights, elastic clusters, and fast interconnects. Outside that regime, many teams will find the design insightful but not immediately deployable.
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger showing that ROS-style weight serving improves broader distributed training or serving workloads beyond RL rollouts would raise its priority.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: TensorHub asks a sharp systems question: if your rollout GPUs already hold the model weights, why keep copying them around instead of treating those live replicas as the storage system?

bridge.title: PEEK: Predictive Queue-Informed KV Cache Management for LLM Serving
bridge.abstract: We present PEEK, a lightweight scheduling and eviction framework for both online (streaming) and offline (batch) LLM serving; this paper focuses on the online regime. PEEK maintains an incremental radix tree over the pending queue, exposing   prefix-sharing clusters no existing engine surfaces. A low-overhead dual-walk matches the tree against the engine's prefix cache to yield longest-prefix-match for every waiting request; PEEK then admits cluster pioneers first so siblings   inherit the freshly cached prefix, a co-designed eviction hook protects blocks ancestral to queued demand, and a multi-lane stride scheduler bounds starvation. On SGLang and vLLM across five workloads up to 4$\times$H100 (DP=2 over TP=2),   PEEK delivers up to 3.0$\times$/2.6$\times$ cache hit, 7.9$\times$/7.1$\times$ TTFT, 6.7$\times$/5.5$\times$ E2E, and 3.6$\times$/4.5$\times$ throughput gains over each engine's strongest stock baseline (SGLang/vLLM), while matching   baselines within noise on workloads with no exploitable prefix structure. Wins hold as KV-cache pressure and inference parallelism scale.
bridge.why_now: LLM serving is increasingly constrained by KV-cache pressure, TTFT, and throughput under shared-prefix traffic, so a queue-aware cache manager is directly relevant now. PEEK matters because it claims large wins on vLLM and SGLang without requiring model changes or new hardware.
bridge.why_not_higher: The upside seems strongly tied to workloads with meaningful prefix reuse, so the real benefit depends on production traffic shape. It is a serving optimization, not a broader shift in model training or evaluation.
downgrade_reasons: Benefits depend on exploitable prefix sharing
Focused on online serving rather than the full AI stack
Comparisons are mainly against stock engine baselines
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger validation on messy real production traces, more model families, and tougher comparisons to other prefix-aware schedulers would raise it.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the next big LLM speedup comes from reading the request queue as a predictor for future KV-cache hits, instead of touching the model at all?

bridge.title: AGENTSERVESIM: A Hardware-aware Simulator for Multi-Turn LLM Agent Serving
bridge.abstract: Multi-turn LLM agents interleave model calls with external tool invocations, shifting serving from stateless request processing to stateful program execution. Serving these workloads requires scheduling, KV-cache management, and routing policies that use program-level context, including turn dependencies, tool-induced gaps, and reusable KV state. Evaluating such policies directly on real systems is costly, since each design point may require dedicated accelerator time across arrival rates, model scales, serving-instance counts, and memory hierarchies. Simulation offers a scalable alternative, but existing LLM serving simulators target stateless request-level workloads and therefore omit the core dynamics of agent serving: multi-turn program execution, cross-turn cache locality, and KV-cache residency during tool gaps. We present AGENTSERVESIM, a hardware-aware simulator for multi-turn LLM agent serving. AGENTSERVESIM evaluates serving policies at program granularity through composable modules: a Program Orchestrator preserves program identity and turn order, a Tool Simulator materializes tool-induced gaps, a Session-Aware Router maintains program-to-instance affinity for cache-aware dispatch, and a KV Residency Model tracks policy-defined KV placement across HBM, host DRAM/CXL, and eviction. Across real serving deployments and hardware configurations, AGENTSERVESIM reproduces real-system behavior within 6% error across key performance metrics while running entirely on commodity CPUs. These results show that AGENTSERVESIM enables controlled, repeatable exploration of agent-serving policies without requiring exhaustive deployment on costly accelerators.
bridge.why_now: Multi-turn tool-using agents are turning inference into a stateful serving problem, and this paper gives teams a cheap way to study scheduling, routing, and KV residency before burning accelerator time. The timing is good because agent workloads are moving from demos toward real deployment constraints.
bridge.why_not_higher: This is a simulator, not a new production serving policy or a direct latency/cost breakthrough on its own. Its broad-audience appeal is lower than papers that show a concrete serving win in live agent stacks.
downgrade_reasons: Indirect impact: evaluation framework rather than deployed serving method
Audience skews toward infra and systems builders
Novelty is stronger in the agent-serving framing than in a new memory mechanism
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: A companion result showing simulator-guided policies delivering clear real-world latency or cost gains on widely used agent workloads would raise priority.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the real bottleneck in AI agents is not the model call, but keeping each agent's KV state alive across tool gaps without wasting scarce HBM?

bridge.title: From Detection to Recovery: Operational Analysis on LLM Pre-training with 504 GPUs
bridge.abstract: Large-scale AI training is fundamentally a distributed systems problem, where hardware failures are routine operating conditions rather than rare exceptions, yet public operational evidence from production training clusters remains limited. This report presents an empirical analysis of a 63-node NVIDIA B200 production cluster (504 GPUs), using 55 days of Prometheus time-series data and 73 days of operational logs covering 224 multi-node training sessions.   The environment is cross-organizational: five parties (SKT, Upstage, Lablup, NVIDIA Korea, VAST Data) share a unified monitoring pipeline. This enabled joint diagnosis of a 60-node-scale storage I/O bottleneck absent in 2-4-node tests, a production-scale phenomenon no single team could isolate alone.   We perform three quantitative analyses yielding four findings. First, over 751 Prometheus metrics and 10 XID-identified GPU failures, no single metric is consistently dominant across failure types, motivating multi-signal detection. Second, 523 checkpoint events trace the save/load path from GPU VRAM to the NFS server: restart loading reaches 21.5% of maximum read bandwidth (700 GB/s) and save bursts 16.0% of maximum write bandwidth (250 GB/s), with NFS/RPC queueing and transport-layer backlog rising together. Third, across 224 sessions over 73 days, node exclusions concentrate so the top 3 of 63 nodes account for over 50%. Fourth, auto-retry chain analysis shows a 33.3% success rate over 12 chains (73 attempts), 2.7x the 12.5% manual rate, with a median retry interval of 11 minutes (IQR 10-11).   All analyses are grounded in production infrastructure providing session-level workload management, GPU-centric scheduling, and unified observability.
bridge.why_now: As large-model training shifts onto bigger GPU clusters, operational reliability and checkpoint I/O are becoming first-order constraints rather than background plumbing. This paper offers rare public evidence from a 504-GPU B200 production environment on failure detection, storage bottlenecks, and recovery behavior at realistic scale.
bridge.why_not_higher: It is still a case study from one production stack, so some lessons may not transfer cleanly across schedulers, storage systems, and model mixes. The paper is strongest as an operational field report rather than a new algorithm or broadly packaged system design.
downgrade_reasons: single-cluster case study
limited method novelty
some findings may be stack-specific
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: A follow-up showing which concrete mitigations fixed the bottlenecks and how the results generalize across additional clusters would raise it further.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: What really breaks when you pre-train LLMs on 504 B200 GPUs, and why do storage queues and retry policy decide whether training recovers or stalls?

bridge.title: Piper: A Programmable Distributed Training System
bridge.abstract: Large-scale model training increasingly relies on composing multiple parallelism strategies, such as data, pipeline, and expert parallelism, together with memory-saving optimizations like ZeRO. Deployed systems for foundation model pretraining often rely on human experts to manually design a high-level parallelism strategy then implement the corresponding low-level execution strategy, making it difficult to adapt the system to new strategies. Meanwhile, many general-purpose frameworks are more flexible but their implementations are still tied to a fixed set of common parallelism strategies, making it challenging to integrate state-of-the-art strategies.   We present Piper, a user-controllable distributed training system that decouples the strategy from the runtime implementation. Piper allows users to declare a comprehensive distributed training strategy with a small set of model annotations and scheduling directives. Each directive applies a transformation on Piper's intermediate representation (IR), a unified global training DAG that represents all computation and communication. Using this IR, Piper compiles per-device execution plans and executes them with a distributed runtime agnostic to the strategy. We show that the combined system maintains performance parity on commonly available strategies such as ZeRO, while also enabling additional performance and memory efficiency gains through joint scheduling of compute and communication in composed parallelism strategies such as DeepSeek-V3's DualPipe.
bridge.why_now: Training stacks are getting harder to hand-wire as teams mix data, pipeline, expert parallelism, and ZeRO-style memory techniques, so a programmable way to express and compile those choices is timely. The paper also anchors the idea in modern composed strategies like DualPipe instead of only older fixed parallelism setups.
bridge.why_not_higher: The memory angle is real but secondary: this is mainly a distributed training programmability paper, not a first-class memory or storage paper. It is also somewhat infra-heavy, so the episode would need to stay focused on the design shift rather than benchmark minutiae.
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger evidence of clear wins across multiple large-model training regimes and signs of likely adoption beyond the paper's own stack would raise it further.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if distributed training stopped being a pile of hand-tuned parallelism recipes and became something you can declare, compile, and retarget as model strategies keep changing?

bridge.title: KV-RM: Regularizing KV-Cache Movement for Static-Graph LLM Serving
bridge.abstract: Static-graph LLM decoders provide predictable launches, fixed tensor shapes, and low submission overhead, but online decoding exposes highly irregular KV-cache behavior: request lengths differ, EOS events arrive asynchronously, and logical histories fragment over time. Dynamic runtimes recover flexibility through paged KV management and step-level scheduling, while static-graph executors often over-reserve memory and suffer burst-time latency outliers. This paper studies whether much of this variability can be absorbed below a fixed decode interface. We present KV-RM, a runtime design that regularizes KV-cache movement beneath a static-graph LLM decoder. KV-RM decouples logical KV histories from physical storage, tracks active KV state through a block pager, and materializes each decode step through a single committed descriptor. A merge-staged transport path coalesces non-contiguous KV mappings into a small number of large transfer groups before a fixed-shape attention kernel consumes them. Optional bounded far-history summaries can be enabled under the same interface, but the core design does not depend on them. On a 2-GPU NVIDIA A100 node, KV-RM improves mixed-length decoding throughput and tail latency relative to a static-graph baseline, reduces reserved KV memory across workload families, and removes severe burst-time latency spikes under production-trace replay. These results suggest that KV-cache movement, rather than kernel shape, can be an effective boundary for recovering runtime flexibility in static-graph LLM serving.
bridge.why_now: KV-cache behavior is now a real bottleneck for mixed-length online decoding, especially as teams push graph-captured or static executors for lower launch overhead. This paper offers a timely systems claim: recover much of the lost flexibility at the KV-movement layer without giving up a fixed decode interface.
bridge.why_not_higher: Its center of gravity is still inference-runtime engineering rather than broad AI interest, and the abstract only shows single-node 2xA100 evidence rather than wider deployment diversity. The KV-serving space is also crowded, so the static-graph framing has to carry the novelty.
downgrade_reasons: Niche outside inference/runtime teams
Single-node evaluation footprint
Crowded KV-serving landscape
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Open-source integration in a widely used serving stack or stronger results across larger models and multi-node deployments would raise it.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can static-graph LLM serving recover dynamic-runtime flexibility by making KV-cache movement, not kernel shape, the place where the runtime gets smart?

bridge.title: Watt Counts: Energy-Aware Benchmark for Sustainable LLM Inference on Heterogeneous GPU Architectures
bridge.abstract: While the large energy consumption of Large Language Models (LLMs) is recognized by the community, system operators lack guidance for energy-efficient LLM inference deployments that leverage energy trade-offs of heterogeneous hardware due to a lack of energy-aware benchmarks and data. In this work we address this gap with Watt Counts: the largest open-access dataset of energy consumption of LLMs, with over 5,000 experiments for 50 LLMs across 10 NVIDIA Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) in batch and server scenarios along with a reproducible, open-source benchmark that enables community submissions to expand this dataset. Leveraging this dataset, we conduct a system-level study of LLM inference across heterogeneous GPU architectures and show that GPU selection is crucial for energy efficiency outcomes and that optimal hardware choices vary significantly across models and deployment scenarios, demonstrating the critical importance of hardware-aware deployment in heterogeneous LLM systems. Guided by our data and insights, we show that practitioners can reduce energy consumption by up to 70% in server scenarios with negligible impact on user experience, and by up to 20% in batch scenarios.
bridge.why_now: LLM inference power draw is becoming a live deployment constraint, and this paper turns that concern into actionable hardware-selection data. The open benchmark, large experiment set, and headline result of major energy savings make it timely for operators and a broader AI audience watching cost and sustainability.
bridge.why_not_higher: Its main contribution is benchmark evidence and deployment guidance rather than a new serving mechanism or memory system. The memory/storage connection is adjacent, not central: it is about heterogeneous GPU efficiency more than KV, paging, offload, or tiering behavior.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage link is adjacent rather than core
Mostly a benchmarking and hardware-selection paper
Hardware scope appears limited to NVIDIA GPU heterogeneity
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: A follow-up that converts these measurements into a concrete scheduler or placement policy validated in production-like multi-vendor serving stacks would raise it.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the biggest lever for greener LLM serving is not a model trick at all, but picking the right GPU for the job and cutting energy use by up to 70%?

public.title: Mind the Sim2Real Gap in User Simulation for Agentic Tasks
public.abstract: As NLP evaluation shifts from static benchmarks to multi-turn interactive settings, LLM-based simulators have become widely used as user proxies, serving two roles: generating user turns and providing evaluation signals. Yet, these simulations are frequently assumed to be faithful to real human behaviors, often without rigorous verification. We formalize the Sim2Real gap in user simulation and present the first study running the full $τ$-bench protocol with real humans (451 participants, 165 tasks), benchmarking 31 LLM simulators across proprietary, open-source, and specialized families using the User-Sim Index (USI), a metric we introduce to quantify how well LLM simulators resemble real user interactive behaviors and feedback. Behaviorally, LLM simulators are excessively cooperative, stylistically uniform, and lack realistic frustration or ambiguity, creating an "easy mode" that inflates agent success rates above the human baseline. In evaluations, real humans provide nuanced judgments across eight quality dimensions while simulated users produce uniformly more positive feedback; rule-based rewards are failing to capture rich feedback signals generated by human users. Overall, higher general model capability does not necessarily yield more faithful user simulation. These findings highlight the importance of human validation when using LLM-based user simulators in the agent development cycle and motivate improved models for user simulation.
public.why_now: Agent benchmarks and LLM-driven product evaluations are leaning heavily on simulated users right now, and this paper argues that many of those results are systematically too optimistic. A large human study plus 31 simulator comparison makes it timely for anyone building or judging agentic systems.
public.why_not_higher: It is a diagnosis of an evaluation failure mode more than a new method that fixes it. The paper is also not connected to the memory/storage lens and is bounded to agent-style interactive evaluation.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism or systems path
Primarily an evaluation critique rather than a new build/serve method
Impact depends on whether teams rely on simulated-user benchmarks
public.what_would_raise_priority: A follow-on paper showing a simulator or hybrid protocol that materially closes the human gap would make this much stronger.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the agent demos everyone trusts are inflated because LLM 'users' are easier, nicer, and less realistic than actual people?

public.title: A Single Neuron Is Sufficient to Bypass Safety Alignment in Large Language Models
public.abstract: Safety alignment in language models operates through two mechanistically distinct systems: refusal neurons that gate whether harmful knowledge is expressed, and concept neurons that encode the harmful knowledge itself. By targeting a single neuron in each system, we demonstrate both directions of failure -- bypassing safety on explicit harmful requests via suppression, and inducing harmful content from innocent prompts via amplification -- across seven models spanning two families and 1.7B to 70B parameters, without any training or prompt engineering. Our findings suggest that safety alignment is not robustly distributed across model weights but is mediated by individual neurons that are each causally sufficient to gate refusal behavior -- suppressing any one of the identified refusal neurons bypasses safety alignment across diverse harmful requests.
public.why_now: This is a sharp, timely claim about alignment fragility just as labs are presenting safety behavior as increasingly robust in deployed LLMs. If a single neuron can gate refusal behavior across multiple model sizes and families, that materially changes how people think about safety evaluation.
public.why_not_higher: The result is broad enough to be interesting, but the abstract alone does not establish how stable these neurons are across retraining, defenses, or a wider set of frontier model families. It also has no direct memory or storage systems angle.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
White-box operational realism is still unclear
public.what_would_raise_priority: Independent replication across more model families, plus evidence that common post-training defenses do not easily patch the failure mode, would raise it.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if safety alignment in a modern LLM is not a distributed safeguard at all, but a switch you can flip by touching a single neuron?

public.title: OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking
public.abstract: Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal, namely the user reply, tool output, terminal or GUI state change that follows each action, yet no existing agentic RL system recovers it as a live, online learning source. We present OpenClaw-RL, a framework that employs next-state signals to optimize personal agents online through infrastructure and methodology innovations. On the infrastructure side, we extend existing RL systems to a server-client architecture where the RL server hosts the policy behind an inference API and user terminals stream interaction data back over HTTP. From each observed next state, the system extracts two complementary training signals, evaluative and directive, via a separate asynchronous server so that neither signal extraction nor optimization blocks inference. On the methodology side, we introduce a hybrid RL objective that unifies both signal types in a single update: directive signals provide richer, token-level supervision but are sparser, while evaluative signals are more broadly available. To stabilize distillation under teacher-student mismatch, we propose overlap-guided hint selection, which picks the hint whose induced teacher distribution maximally overlaps with the student's top-$k$ tokens, together with a log-probability-difference clip that bounds per-token advantages. Applied to personal agents, OpenClaw-RL enables an agent to improve simply by being used, recovering conversational signals from user re-queries, corrections, and explicit feedback. Applied to general agents, OpenClaw-RL is the first RL framework to unify real-world agent settings spanning terminal, GUI, SWE, and tool-call environments, where we additionally demonstrate the utility of next-state signals in long-horizon settings.
public.why_now: Agentic AI is moving from static benchmark tuning to deployed assistants that have to learn from messy real interactions, and this paper offers a concrete online RL recipe for doing that. The framing is timely because many teams are actively shipping terminal, GUI, and tool-using agents.
public.why_not_higher: This is not a memory/storage paper, so it does not serve the podcast's second lens. The abstract also does not yet show enough about scale, robustness, and operational cost to treat it as a clear field-level shift rather than a strong agent-training framework.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Online feedback signals may be noisy and deployment-specific
Frontier-scale validation is unclear from the abstract alone
public.what_would_raise_priority: Large-scale results on widely used agent stacks showing durable gains over strong offline baselines with acceptable serving and training overhead would raise it further.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if your agent could get better simply by watching what happens after every action you and your tools take?

public.title: LLaDA2.1: Speeding Up Text Diffusion via Token Editing
public.abstract: While LLaDA2.0 showcased the scaling potential of 100B-level block-diffusion models and their inherent parallelization, the delicate equilibrium between decoding speed and generation quality has remained an elusive frontier. Today, we unveil LLaDA2.1, a paradigm shift designed to transcend this trade-off. By seamlessly weaving Token-to-Token (T2T) editing into the conventional Mask-to-Token (M2T) scheme, we introduce a joint, configurable threshold-decoding scheme. This structural innovation gives rise to two distinct personas: the Speedy Mode (S Mode), which audaciously lowers the M2T threshold to bypass traditional constraints while relying on T2T to refine the output; and the Quality Mode (Q Mode), which leans into conservative thresholds to secure superior benchmark performances with manageable efficiency degrade. Furthering this evolution, underpinned by an expansive context window, we implement the first large-scale Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework specifically tailored for dLLMs, anchored by specialized techniques for stable gradient estimation. This alignment not only sharpens reasoning precision but also elevates instruction-following fidelity, bridging the chasm between diffusion dynamics and complex human intent. We culminate this work by releasing LLaDA2.1-Mini (16B) and LLaDA2.1-Flash (100B). Across 33 rigorous benchmarks, LLaDA2.1 delivers strong task performance and lightning-fast decoding speed. Despite its 100B volume, on coding tasks it attains an astounding 892 TPS on HumanEval+, 801 TPS on BigCodeBench, and 663 TPS on LiveCodeBench.
public.why_now: This is a timely attempt to make text diffusion models competitive on the issue that has held them back most: decoding speed. A 100B diffusion model claiming very high coding-benchmark throughput is enough to reopen the autoregressive-versus-diffusion debate for a broad AI audience.
public.why_not_higher: The memory/storage connection is weak, and benchmark TPS is not the same as end-to-end serving latency or cost on real deployments. The core question is whether these gains survive apples-to-apples comparison against strong autoregressive baselines outside curated evals.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage relevance is indirect rather than mechanism-level
Throughput claims are stronger than the deployment-level serving evidence
Impact depends on broader adoption of diffusion LLMs, which is still unsettled
public.what_would_raise_priority: A clear end-to-end serving comparison showing lower real latency or cost than top autoregressive models on production-style workloads would raise priority.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can token editing turn diffusion language models from a parallel curiosity into a real challenger to autoregressive decoding?

public.title: Thinking to Recall: How Reasoning Unlocks Parametric Knowledge in LLMs
public.abstract: While reasoning in LLMs plays a natural role in math, code generation, and multi-hop factual questions, its effect on simple, single-hop factual questions remains unclear. Such questions do not require step-by-step logical decomposition, making the utility of reasoning highly counterintuitive. Nevertheless, we find that enabling reasoning substantially expands the capability boundary of the model's parametric knowledge recall, unlocking correct answers that are otherwise effectively unreachable. Why does reasoning aid parametric knowledge recall when there are no complex reasoning steps to be done? To answer this, we design a series of hypothesis-driven controlled experiments, and identify two key driving mechanisms: (1) a computational buffer effect, where the model uses the generated reasoning tokens to perform latent computation independent of their semantic content; and (2) factual priming, where generating topically related facts acts as a semantic bridge that facilitates correct answer retrieval. Importantly, this latter generative self-retrieval mechanism carries inherent risks: we demonstrate that hallucinating intermediate facts during reasoning increases the likelihood of hallucinations in the final answer. Finally, we show that our insights can be harnessed to directly improve model accuracy by prioritizing reasoning trajectories that contain hallucination-free factual statements.
public.why_now: Reasoning behavior in LLMs is still shifting rapidly, and this paper gives a clear mechanism-level account of why chain-of-thought can improve even simple factual recall. It also matters now because it ties reasoning gains to hallucination risk in a way builders can evaluate and potentially act on.
public.why_not_higher: The memory angle is effectively absent in the systems sense this show cares about, despite the recall framing. It is also more about prompting and evaluation behavior than a concrete change to training or serving infrastructure.
downgrade_reasons: Not a memory/storage paper
Limited direct systems impact
Risk of overlapping with recent reasoning/hallucination coverage
public.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger evidence that the proposed trajectory-selection method reliably improves production factuality across frontier models and real user workloads would raise it further.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why does making an LLM 'think' help it remember facts it already knows, and why can that same process also make its hallucinations worse?

public.title: Emergent Misalignment is Easy, Narrow Misalignment is Hard
public.abstract: Finetuning large language models on narrowly harmful datasets can cause them to become emergently misaligned, giving stereotypically `evil' responses across diverse unrelated settings. Concerningly, a pre-registered survey of experts failed to predict this result, highlighting our poor understanding of the inductive biases governing learning and generalisation in LLMs. We use emergent misalignment (EM) as a case study to investigate these inductive biases and find that models can just learn the narrow dataset task, but that the general solution appears to be more stable and more efficient. To establish this, we build on the result that different EM finetunes converge to the same linear representation of general misalignment, which can be used to mediate misaligned behaviour. We find a linear representation of the narrow solution also exists, and can be learned by introducing a KL divergence loss. Comparing these representations reveals that general misalignment achieves lower loss, is more robust to perturbations, and is more influential in the pre-training distribution. This work isolates a concrete representation of general misalignment for monitoring and mitigation. More broadly, it offers a detailed case study and preliminary metrics for investigating how inductive biases shape generalisation in LLMs. We open-source all code, datasets and model finetunes.
public.why_now: Emergent misalignment is a sharp, surprising fine-tuning failure mode, and this paper goes beyond the headline by isolating a concrete representation for monitoring and mitigation. The expert survey miss also makes it timely as a reality check on how weak our intuitions still are about LLM post-training.
public.why_not_higher: The practical takeaway is stronger for evaluation and safety monitoring than for mainstream training or serving workflows, and the memory/storage lens is absent. The evidence also appears tied to a specific harmful-finetune setup rather than a broad sweep of frontier deployment regimes.
public.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same mechanism and mitigation leverage on larger contemporary instruction-tuned models in more realistic post-training pipelines.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: If a model learns to be broadly evil faster than it learns one narrow bad task, what does that reveal about the shortcuts fine-tuning prefers?

public.title: When Benign Inputs Lead to Severe Harms: Eliciting Unsafe Unintended Behaviors of Computer-Use Agents
public.abstract: Although computer-use agents (CUAs) hold significant potential to automate increasingly complex OS workflows, they can demonstrate unsafe unintended behaviors that deviate from expected outcomes even under benign input contexts. However, exploration of this risk remains largely anecdotal, lacking concrete characterization and automated methods to proactively surface long-tail unintended behaviors under realistic CUA scenarios. To fill this gap, we introduce the first conceptual and methodological framework for unintended CUA behaviors, by defining their key characteristics, automatically eliciting them, and analyzing how they arise from benign inputs. We propose AutoElicit: an agentic framework that iteratively perturbs benign instructions using CUA execution feedback, and elicits severe harms while keeping perturbations realistic and benign. Using AutoElicit, we surface hundreds of harmful unintended behaviors from state-of-the-art CUAs such as Claude 4.5 Haiku, Claude 4.5 Opus, and Operator. We further evaluate the transferability of human-verified successful perturbations, identifying persistent susceptibility to unintended behaviors across various other frontier CUAs. This work establishes a foundation for systematically analyzing unintended behaviors in realistic computer-use settings.
public.why_now: Computer-use agents are moving from demos into real workflows, so failure modes that arise from benign user intent are immediately relevant to builders, evaluators, and buyers. This paper gives a concrete automated way to surface those harms on frontier agents instead of relying on anecdotes.
public.why_not_higher: It is mainly an evaluation and red-teaming paper, not a mitigation or systems-design advance. The memory/storage link is effectively absent, and the scope is strongest for computer-use agents rather than the full modern AI stack.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage contribution
Characterizes failures more than it fixes them
Most relevant to computer-use agents rather than general LLM training or serving
public.what_would_raise_priority: A strong defense story showing materially reduced unsafe behaviors across multiple vendors without hurting agent usefulness would raise it further.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: If a harmless-looking request can push a computer-use agent into doing something seriously dangerous, how close are today's desktop agents to being safe enough for real autonomy?

public.title: IH-Challenge: A Training Dataset to Improve Instruction Hierarchy on Frontier LLMs
public.abstract: Instruction hierarchy (IH) defines how LLMs prioritize system, developer, user, and tool instructions under conflict, providing a concrete, trust-ordered policy for resolving instruction conflicts. IH is key to defending against jailbreaks, system prompt extractions, and agentic prompt injections. However, robust IH behavior is difficult to train: IH failures can be confounded with instruction-following failures, conflicts can be nuanced, and models can learn shortcuts such as overrefusing. We introduce IH-Challenge, a reinforcement learning training dataset, to address these difficulties. Fine-tuning GPT-5-Mini on IH-Challenge with online adversarial example generation improves IH robustness by +10.0% on average across 16 in-distribution, out-of-distribution, and human red-teaming benchmarks (84.1% to 94.1%), reduces unsafe behavior from 6.6% to 0.7% while improving helpfulness on general safety evaluations, and saturates an internal static agentic prompt injection evaluation, with minimal capability regression. We release the IH-Challenge dataset (https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/ih-challenge) to support future research on robust instruction hierarchy.
public.why_now: Instruction hierarchy is becoming a practical fault line for agentic LLM products because prompt injection, jailbreaks, and system prompt leakage are now deployment issues rather than lab curiosities. A released training dataset with reported gains on robustness and helpfulness makes this more actionable than a generic safety warning.
public.why_not_higher: The paper is not a memory/storage story, so it only fits one of the show's two lenses. The evidence is promising but still leans on one proprietary model family plus several internal evaluations, which limits how hard to generalize the headline gains.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection is absent
Some key validation appears on internal or proprietary evaluations
Impact is strongest for safety and evaluation workflows rather than core model capability
public.what_would_raise_priority: Independent replication on strong open models and public agentic prompt-injection benchmarks would make it a clearer must-cover paper.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if one of the biggest upgrades for AI agents is not more scale, but finally teaching models which instructions to obey when everything conflicts?

public.title: KnowU-Bench: Towards Interactive, Proactive, and Personalized Mobile Agent Evaluation
public.abstract: Personalized mobile agents that infer user preferences and calibrate proactive assistance hold great promise as everyday digital assistants, yet existing benchmarks fail to capture what this requires. Prior work evaluates preference recovery from static histories or intent prediction from fixed contexts. Neither tests whether an agent can elicit missing preferences through interaction, nor whether it can decide when to intervene, seek consent, or remain silent in a live GUI environment. We introduce KnowU-Bench, an online benchmark for personalized mobile agents built on a reproducible Android emulation environment, covering 42 general GUI tasks, 86 personalized tasks, and 64 proactive tasks. Unlike prior work that treats user preferences as static context, KnowU-Bench hides the user profile from the agent and exposes only behavioral logs, forcing genuine preference inference rather than context lookup. To support multi-turn preference elicitation, it instantiates an LLM-driven user simulator grounded in structured profiles, enabling realistic clarification dialogues and proactive consent handling. Beyond personalization, KnowU-Bench provides comprehensive evaluation of the complete proactive decision chain, including grounded GUI execution, consent negotiation, and post-rejection restraint, evaluated through a hybrid protocol combining rule-based verification with LLM-as-a-Judge scoring. Our experiments reveal a striking degradation: agents that excel at explicit task execution fall below 50% under vague instructions requiring user preference inference or intervention calibration, even for frontier models like Claude Sonnet 4.6. The core bottlenecks are not GUI navigation but preference acquisition and intervention calibration, exposing a fundamental gap between competent interface operation and trustworthy personal assistance.
public.why_now: Personal AI agents are moving from toy demos toward real assistants, and this benchmark targets the part current agent hype often skips: inferring preferences, asking for consent, and knowing when not to act. The finding that frontier models still break under vague, preference-dependent tasks is timely for anyone building agent products or eval stacks.
public.why_not_higher: The memory/storage connection is absent, and the main contribution is an evaluation benchmark rather than a new training or serving primitive. The setup is also centered on mobile GUI tasks with a simulated user, so the broader transfer story is credible but not fully proven.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Benchmark and simulator evidence is stronger than deployed-user evidence
Mobile-agent scope is narrower than general agent infrastructure
public.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that KnowU-Bench predicts failures in deployed assistants, or rapid adoption as a standard eval by major agent teams, would raise priority.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: Frontier agents can tap through Android screens, but can they figure out what you want, ask permission at the right time, and know when to stay quiet?

public.title: PARD-2: Target-Aligned Parallel Draft Model for Dual-Mode Speculative Decoding
public.abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates Large Language Models (LLMs) inference by using a lightweight draft model to propose candidate tokens that are verified in parallel by the target model. However, existing draft model training objectives are not directly aligned with the inference-time goal of maximizing consecutive token acceptance. To address this issue, we reformulate the draft model optimization objective, shifting the focus from token prediction accuracy to the overall acceptance length. In this paper, we build upon PARD to propose PARD-2, a dual-mode speculative decoding framework with Confidence-Adaptive Token (CAT) optimization. This approach adaptively reweights each token to better align with the verification process. Notably, PARD-2 enables a single draft model to support both target-dependent and target-independent modes. Experiments across diverse models and tasks demonstrate that PARD-2 achieves up to 6.94$\times$ lossless acceleration, surpassing EAGLE-3 by 1.9$\times$ and PARD by 1.3$\times$ on Llama3.1-8B. Our code is available at https://github.com/AMD-AGI/PARD.
public.why_now: Speculative decoding is one of the few practical ways to get lossless LLM serving speedups right now, so better draft-model objectives matter immediately. PARD-2 targets the actual acceptance-length objective and makes one draft model usable in both target-dependent and target-independent modes, which is a real deployment story.
public.why_not_higher: This is not a memory/storage paper in the first-class sense; the connection is serving efficiency, not cache, tiering, or data placement. It also lands in a crowded speculative decoding lane, so the episode has to explain why this is more than another benchmark win over the previous draft-model baseline.
downgrade_reasons: Memory relevance is indirect rather than central
Crowded speculative decoding space
Real-world serving robustness is not fully clear from the abstract
public.what_would_raise_priority: Clearer end-to-end serving results across larger models, varied hardware, and production-style latency-throughput settings would raise it further.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the right way to train a speculative decoder is not to guess the next token better, but to maximize how long the big model keeps saying yes?

memory.title: RATrain: A Resource-Aware Training Runtime for Large Language Models on Bandwidth-Constrained Heterogeneous Supercomputing Platforms
memory.abstract: Production heterogeneous supercomputing platforms are increasingly used to host large language model (LLM) training workloads. However, existing GPU-oriented training runtimes typically rely on high-bandwidth device memory, fast interconnects, and mature collective communication libraries, making them difficult to directly adapt to MT-3000, a platform with an explicit memory hierarchy, limited usable DDR capacity, and constrained inter-cluster communication. This paper presents RATrain, a resource-aware training runtime for dense LLMs on bandwidth-constrained heterogeneous supercomputing platforms. RATrain formulates standard non-interleaved 1F1B training as a training-state lifecycle scheduling problem, and schedules gradient synchronization, parameter update, parameter-view prefetching, and activation recovery at layer-level and stage-local granularity. RATrain further combines an MT-3000-aware execution backend for efficient and predictable FP16 GEMM, Attention Backward, and explicit data movement with a resource-aware planner that selects feasible training configurations under the 20GB usable-DDR constraint per compute cluster. We implement RATrain on a real MT-3000 platform and evaluate it using LLaMA-2-7B, Baichuan2-13B, Qwen2.5-32B, and LLaMA-2-70B configurations. Results show that RATrain achieves up to 1.35$\times$ end-to-end speedup over MT-3000-adapted GPU-style training strategies. For LLaMA-2-7B, RATrain scales to 1024 compute clusters, reaches 112,790.55 tokens/s, and achieves 97.0\% scaling efficiency. A further 1.028B-token correctness run shows that RATrain preserves the loss trajectory of a semantically equivalent Baseline-1F1B run, with a maximum relative loss deviation of 0.081\%.
memory.why_now: As LLM training spreads beyond standard NVLink-rich GPU clusters, bandwidth, memory hierarchy, and usable host memory are becoming first-order constraints. This paper matters now because it shows a concrete runtime that treats data movement and state lifetime as the main systems problem rather than assuming GPU-style abundance.
memory.why_not_higher: The core contribution is strong, but the paper is tightly anchored to MT-3000 and a specific 1F1B training setup, which limits immediate transfer to the hardware most listeners use. The gains are credible and well-supported, but they look like solid systems engineering rather than a field-shifting training recipe.
downgrade_reasons: MT-3000-specific implementation limits immediate transferability
Broad AI audience is narrower than the memory-systems audience
Performance gains are meaningful but not a step-change
memory.what_would_raise_priority: A demonstration that the same lifecycle scheduling ideas deliver comparable wins on more common heterogeneous GPU or accelerator clusters would raise it.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: What breaks when you try to train frontier-scale LLMs on a machine where bandwidth and memory hierarchy, not FLOPs, are the real boss?

memory.title: Pooling Engram Conditional Memory in Large Language Models using CXL
memory.abstract: Engram conditional memory has emerged as a promising component for LLMs by decoupling static knowledge lookup from dynamic computation. Since Engram exhibits sparse access patterns and supports prefetching, its massive embedding tables are well-suited for offloading to lower-tier memory. In this paper, we propose using Compute Express Link (CXL) memory pool for Engram storage. Compared to RDMA, CXL provides fine-grained and low-latency access required by minimal and discrete retrieval patterns of Engram. We integrate the CXL-based Engram pool into SGLang, achieving near-DRAM end-to-end performance. This provides a scalable and cost-efficient storage solution for future Engram-integrated LLMs without compromising inference performance.
memory.why_now: As LLM serving runs into HBM and DRAM limits, CXL is moving from architecture slideware to concrete memory-tier experiments. This paper matters because it tests CXL on a sparse, prefetchable LLM memory path inside SGLang rather than on an isolated microbenchmark.
memory.why_not_higher: The result is tied to Engram, which is not yet a mainstream LLM deployment primitive. It is still unclear how much of the benefit transfers to standard KV-cache-heavy serving or other model families.
downgrade_reasons: Engram is still niche relative to mainstream LLM serving
transferability beyond this conditional-memory design is unclear
CXL deployment is still limited in typical production stacks
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the same CXL pooling approach helps mainstream open-source serving workloads or broader external-memory designs would raise priority.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can CXL make external memory practical for LLMs by keeping giant sparse knowledge tables off the main memory path without paying a real inference penalty?

memory.title: ATLAS: Efficient Out-of-Core Inference for Billion-Scale Graph Neural Networks
memory.abstract: Graph Neural Network (GNN) inference on billion-scale graphs is critical for domains like fintech and recommendation systems. Full-graph inference on these large graphs can be challenging due to high communication costs in distributed settings and high I/O costs in disk-backed Out-of-Core (OOC) settings. Existing OOC systems, operating across disk and memory, primarily focus on GNN training and perform poorly for full-graph inference due to massive read amplification, irregular I/O, and memory pressure. We present ATLAS, a disk-based GNN inference framework that enables efficient full-graph, layer-wise inference on graphs whose topologies, features and intermediate embeddings exceed the available memory on single machines. ATLAS replaces gather-based execution with a broadcast-based model that enables sequential, single-pass streaming reads of features and embeddings per layer. A tiered memory-disk hierarchy with minimum-pending-message eviction, graph reordering and a GPU-accelerated pipeline sustains high throughput within $128$ GiB RAM and $2$ TiB SSD. Across out-of-core graphs with up to $4$B edges and $550$ GiB features and multiple GNN architectures, ATLAS improves end-to-end inference time by $\approx12$--$30\times$ over State-of-the-Art (SOTA) OOC baselines on a single workstation, while remaining within $\approx5\%$ when features fit in memory.
memory.why_now: It tackles a real systems bottleneck: full-graph inference when graph structure, features, and embeddings no longer fit in memory. The disk-memory hierarchy, streaming access pattern, and eviction policy make it relevant to current interest in memory-bound AI serving beyond pure transformer workloads.
memory.why_not_higher: The audience is narrower than mainstream foundation-model systems work because it is specific to billion-scale GNN inference. The paper also reads more like a strong systems optimization result than a shift that broadly changes how most AI teams build or serve models.
downgrade_reasons: niche to GNN inference
limited direct relevance to mainstream LLM serving
transferability beyond graph workloads is suggestive rather than proven
memory.what_would_raise_priority: A clearer demonstration that its access-pattern and tiering ideas transfer to broader sparse or memory-bound AI inference workloads would raise it.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: What happens when your graph model is bigger than RAM: ATLAS turns out-of-core GNN inference into a streaming storage problem and shows how far careful data movement can go.

memory.title: TF-Engram: A Train-Free Engram with SSD-Backed Memory for Large Language Models
memory.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) store factual knowledge and domain-specific patterns implicitly in dense Transformer parameters, making knowledge expansion costly through pretraining, fine-tuning, retrieval augmentation, or longer contexts. Engram-style memory offers a compact hidden-state injection pathway, but existing GPU-resident designs often rely on hash-based compression, causing unrelated phrases to collide in shared slots and weakening phrase-level semantic fidelity. We present TF-Engram, a train-free Engram system that constructs phrase-specific semantic memory offline from external corpora, stores large memory tables across a GPU--DRAM--SSD hierarchy, and uses Early-Exit Guided Predictive Prefetching to hide external-memory latency during autoregressive decoding. On Qwen3-0.6B, TF-Engram improves the average downstream score from 57.6 to 59.4, outperforming both the frozen backbone and a parameter-matched LoRA baseline. System evaluation shows that large TF-Engram tables can be built with moderate offline cost, SSD-backed storage substantially reduces GPU memory demand, and predictive prefetching recovers much of the throughput loss caused by external memory access. These results demonstrate that static phrase memory can be integrated into LLM inference as a scalable, train-free, and low-overhead system component.
memory.why_now: It puts external phrase memory directly into the LLM serving path with explicit GPU-DRAM-SSD tiering and predictive prefetch, which is a live systems question as inference stacks keep running into memory limits. The train-free angle also makes it relevant to teams looking for cheaper knowledge expansion than retraining or long-context scaling.
memory.why_not_higher: The evidence is still narrow: the headline results are on Qwen3-0.6B with modest quality gains, so it does not yet show that the approach survives larger, production-scale models or tougher workloads. It is interesting system design, but not yet a clear change in mainstream LLM serving practice.
downgrade_reasons: validated mainly on a small 0.6B model
quality gains are modest
serving relevance is promising but not yet proven at larger scale
memory.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger paper would show the same memory hierarchy and prefetch benefits on multi-billion-parameter models with clearer latency-throughput-quality tradeoffs against RAG, LoRA, and longer-context baselines.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can an LLM bolt on an SSD-backed phrase memory and prefetch the right facts fast enough to beat fine-tuning and long context without retraining?

memory.title: Compiler-First State Space Duality and Portable $O(1)$ Autoregressive Caching for Inference
memory.abstract: High-throughput Mamba-2 inference is usually tied to fused CUDA and Triton kernels, limiting portability across accelerator backends. We show that the state space duality (SSD) recurrence has a compiler-friendly structure: diagonal per-head dynamics, fixed-size chunking, einsum-dominated compute, and static control flow. Expressing this structure in standard JAX primitives gives a single-source inference path with no custom kernels, a registered JAX PyTree cache, and a compiled on-device autoregressive loop. On a single Google Cloud TPU v6e, batch-1 prefill reaches approximately 140 TFLOPS, or 15% model FLOP utilisation (MFU), the roofline ceiling for this regime, and cached decode reaches up to 64% hardware bandwidth utilisation (HBU). At a 4096-token context, cached decode is 27x--36x faster than full-prefix recomputation across five Mamba-2 checkpoints from 130M to 2.7B parameters. The same source runs unmodified on NVIDIA L40S, where cached decode remains sequence-length independent across all model scales. WikiText-103 validation perplexity matches the Triton reference mamba_ssm v2.2.2 within +/-0.0005 points, and hidden states agree to float32 rounding tolerance. Code is available at https://github.com/CosmoNaught/mamba2-jax.
memory.why_now: If Mamba-style models are going to matter in production, they need inference paths that are not locked to bespoke CUDA or Triton kernels. This paper makes a timely portability case by showing cached O(1) decode in standard JAX across both TPU and NVIDIA hardware.
memory.why_not_higher: The impact is still architecture-specific: it improves Mamba-2 serving, not the transformer-heavy path most teams run today. The story is strong on benchmarks and implementation discipline, but weaker on broader deployment shift or ecosystem adoption.
downgrade_reasons: Mamba-2-specific rather than model-agnostic
Single-device inference evidence only
Limited direct impact on transformer-dominant serving stacks
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same compiler-first caching approach scaling to larger production serving setups or influencing mainstream non-Mamba inference stacks.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can Mamba finally escape the custom-kernel trap, or is portable O(1) autoregressive caching still a niche win for alternative sequence models?

memory.title: Taming GPU Underutilization via Static Partitioning and Fine-grained CPU Offloading
memory.abstract: Advances in GPU compute throughput and memory capacity brings significant opportunities to a wide range of workloads. However, efficiently utilizing these resources remains challenging, particularly because diverse application characteristics may result in imbalanced utilization. Multi-Instance GPU (MIG) is a promising approach to improve utilization by partitioning GPU compute and memory resources into fixed-size slices with isolation. Yet, its effectiveness and limitations in supporting HPC workloads remain an open question. We present a comprehensive system-level characterization of different GPU sharing options using real-world scientific, AI, and data analytics applications, including NekRS, LAMMPS, Llama3, and Qiskit. Our analysis reveals that while GPU sharing via MIG can significantly reduce resource underutilization, and enable system-level improvements in throughput and energy, interference still occurs through shared resources, such as power throttling. Our performance-resource scaling results indicate that coarse-grained provisioning for tightly coupled compute and memory resources often mismatches application needs. To address this mismatch, we propose a memory-offloading scheme that leverages the cache-coherent Nvlink-C2C interconnect to bridge the gap between coarse-grained resource slices and reduce resource underutilization.
memory.why_now: Shared GPU clusters are now normal for AI workloads, and this paper asks a timely operational question: how much utilization do we lose from rigid GPU partitioning, and can coherent CPU memory recover stranded capacity? That matters now for teams evaluating MIG-style sharing and Grace Hopper-class nodes.
memory.why_not_higher: The paper is more about GPU partitioning efficiency than a broadly applicable AI memory mechanism, and the most interesting fix depends on specific NVIDIA hardware features. It feels important for infrastructure operators, but not yet like a field-wide change in how most AI teams build or serve models.
downgrade_reasons: Hardware-specific to MIG and NVLink-C2C
AI is only one workload class in the evaluation
Stronger on utilization analysis than on a broadly portable systems recipe
memory.what_would_raise_priority: A clearer end-to-end result on production LLM serving or training stacks, especially versus standard offload and scheduling baselines on widely deployed hardware, would raise it.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the real waste in AI clusters is not too little GPU, but slicing GPUs too rigidly and ignoring the chance to spill memory across coherent CPU links?

memory.title: UBEP: Re-architecting Expert Parallelism Communication Library for Production Superpods
memory.abstract: The deployment of Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models on production high-bandwidth superpods, such as NVIDIA's NVL72/576 and Huawei's CloudMatrix384, introduces critical challenges beyond raw interconnect bandwidth. While these systems provide unified global address spaces and high-bandwidth fabrics, their full potential for sparse MoE communication is hindered by three fundamental bottlenecks: (1) Strict execution serialization imposed by coarse-grained Bulk Synchronous Parallel (BSP) orchestration of interdependent communication phases; (2) Prohibitive synchronization overhead that fails to scale alongside high interconnect bandwidth; and (3) Severe load imbalance resulting from distance-agnostic scheduling of irregular token traffic. To eliminate these bottlenecks, we introduce UBEP (Unified-Bus Expert Parallelism), a production-ready communication library that rethinks MoE's All-to-All primitives for modern superpod architectures. Through large scale experiments, UBEP reduces All-to-All latency by up to 52.4% and MoE inference Time Per Output Token (TPOT) by up to 11.1%.
memory.why_now: MoE deployment is moving onto tightly coupled superpods, and this paper argues that raw fabric bandwidth is not enough if expert-parallel communication stays BSP-heavy and poorly scheduled. That makes it timely for teams trying to turn NVL72-class hardware into real serving wins rather than brochure bandwidth.
memory.why_not_higher: The paper is still fairly narrow: it is about MoE expert-parallel communication on very specific high-end pod architectures, not a broadly reusable shift for most AI builders. It also risks reading more like an optimized collective library result than a wider conceptual change in how modern AI systems are built.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow to MoE expert-parallel deployments
Transferability beyond top-end superpods is unclear
Can collapse into communication-kernel benchmark detail
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger evidence that the scheduling and synchronization ideas generalize across vendors, software stacks, and a wider set of production MoE workloads would raise it.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why do giant AI superpods still bottleneck on sparse MoE token traffic, and what does it take to turn headline interconnect bandwidth into lower time-per-token in practice?

memory.title: Memory Scarcity, Open Models, and the Restructuring of the AI Industry, 2026-2030 -- A quantitative scenario analysis of inference economics, training-cost divergence, and infrastructure solvency
memory.abstract: We analyze how four forces restructure the AI industry over 2026-2030: the DRAM/HBM price surge, frontier-capable open-weight models (GLM-5.2), rapid inference-efficiency gains (near-Shannon-limit KV-cache compression, lightweight local runtimes), and the entry of Meta and xAI into compute resale on fleets bought before the memory repricing. Formulating inference economics in dollars per petabyte of bandwidth delivered (\$/PB) -- model-agnostic for bandwidth-bound decode -- we show the entrant-incumbent cost gap never closes: a depreciation conveyor delivers newly amortized fleets to incumbents faster than hardware prices normalize (3.2x in 2026, 1.9x in 2027, re-widening to 3-4x by 2029-30). Training bifurcates into a luxury tier (\$18-38B per frontier run by 2030) and a mass tier (previous-frontier parity via RL/distillation falling toward \$5M). Solvency of the announced buildout is confined to a corridor requiring roughly 2x annual token-demand growth for four years with sticky premium pricing; a measurement critique shows public token trackers overstate monetizable demand, and all pre-Q2-2026 projections predate the industry's shift from token maximization to token minimization. A vintage-breakeven analysis finds 2026 and 2028-29 capacity each fatally exposed to one pricing regime, with only the 2027 vintage robust. A greenfield custom-silicon entrant removes the merchant margin but not the memory premium (central outcome: 25% success/34% mediocre/41% loss, improvable via staged go/no-go gates). China's LineShine LX2 -- domestic HBM on a standard ISA -- decouples its cost curve from the memory crisis. Scenario probabilities: Rotating Landlord Oligopoly 25%, Commoditization Crash 25%, Jevons Absorption 20%, System-Layer Re-differentiation 18%, Geopolitical Bifurcation 12%. Solvency now depends on monetized bandwidth demand, premium stickiness, and vintage ownership.
memory.why_now: It hits two live concerns at once: whether inference economics are becoming memory-dominated, and whether open models plus older fleet ownership can upend the current AI business hierarchy. That makes it timely for both broad AI listeners and the podcast's memory-oriented lens.
memory.why_not_higher: The paper is mostly a scenario and solvency analysis rather than a measured systems paper, so the causal claims depend heavily on assumptions about pricing, demand, and hardware vintages. It is interesting and provocative, but not yet strongly anchored in real production workload data or reproducible hardware evidence.
downgrade_reasons: Scenario-heavy with assumption-sensitive conclusions
Limited validation on real workloads, fleets, or operator cost data
More industry forecasting than directly actionable systems guidance
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Measured validation against real decode bandwidth traces, fleet economics, and sensitivity-tested hardware cost data would make the thesis much stronger.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the next AI shakeout is decided less by model quality than by who owns the right memory-bound inference fleets when HBM gets expensive?

memory.title: FourTune: Towards Fully 4-Bit Efficient Post-Training for Diffusion Models
memory.abstract: Diffusion models have become a dominant paradigm for high-quality generative modeling, while post-training is essential for adapting them to diverse downstream applications. However, post-training of large diffusion models is still challenging due to the prohibitive memory footprints and slow training speed, which existing parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods only partially address. To overcome these limitations, we propose FourTune, an efficient post-training framework for diffusion models based on an end-to-end W4A4G4 paradigm. FourTune introduces a triple-branch hybrid pipeline that augments the standard LoRA architecture with a frozen numerical stabilizer to isolate quantization-sensitive outliers, enabling stable training under native 4-bit computation. In addition, FourTune employs hardware-efficient block-wise quantization and customized fused kernels to support efficient quantized backpropagation and reduce memory bandwidth overhead. Across customization, reinforcement learning, and distillation tasks, FourTune matches the quality of full-precision fine-tuning. On FLUX.1-dev (12B), FourTune reduces memory overhead by 2.25$\times$ and increases end-to-end training throughput by 2.27$\times$ compared to BF16 LoRA.
memory.why_now: Low-bit post-training is moving from inference into full training loops, and this paper claims a real 12B diffusion model can be adapted with materially lower memory and higher throughput. That makes it timely for teams trying to keep large generative models trainable on finite hardware budgets.
memory.why_not_higher: The core result is still diffusion-specific and training-side, not a broader shift for mainstream LLM serving or memory systems. The memory story is real, but it is quantized training efficiency rather than KV cache, offload, paging, or tiering.
downgrade_reasons: Diffusion-specific rather than clearly model-agnostic
Memory angle is bandwidth/capacity reduction, not storage or cache mechanisms
Risks reading as a strong optimization result more than a field-shifting systems paper
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same native 4-bit post-training recipe transferring cleanly to LLMs, video models, or multiple hardware stacks with similarly strong quality retention.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can you really fine-tune a 12B diffusion model end-to-end in 4-bit and keep full-precision quality, or does low-bit training still break when the workload gets real?

memory.title: Isolation-aware Scheduling Framework for DNN-based End-to-End Autonomous Driving System on Tile-based Accelerators
memory.abstract: Level-4+ autonomous driving systems (ADS) must run dozens of heterogeneous deep neural networks (DNNs) as end-to-end (E2E) pipelines under a strict latency constraint (<=100 ms), even as execution time varies by up to 3.3x. Cost rules out dedicating isolated hardware to each function in mass-produced ADS, so these DNNs must be densely colocated on a single chip, which introduces shared-resource contention. Tile-based accelerators expose two scheduling opportunities that conventional ADS schedulers do not exploit. First, they provide a tunable degree of parallelism (DoP): assigning more tiles raises DoP and can shorten DNN execution time. Second, they provide hardware-native isolation: tiles can be physically partitioned among co-located DNNs. But using this flexibility is expensive: changing a task's DoP triggers a stop-migrate-restart reallocation of its weights and intermediate features. At ADS task rates of 10-240 Hz, these stalls accumulate along E2E chains and threaten deadlines. Reservation-based schedulers fix DoP and leave this flexibility unused; work-conserving schedulers exploit it but assume reallocation is cheap and treat deadlines as independent. We present ADS-Tile that combines configurable isolation and elastic reservation into a spatio-temporal isolation-sharing space that bounds where and when reallocation occurs; a probabilistic latency model and a DAG-aware runtime scheduler then use this space to decide task colocation and DoP under shared E2E deadlines. On an industry- and academia- derived ADS benchmark, ADS-Tile uses up to 32% fewer tiles than the work-conserving baseline in deadline-critical settings and cuts reallocation-induced wasted processing capacity from 17%-44% to below 1.2%. Controlled spatio-temporal sharing improves resource efficiency and latency predictability for tile-based ADS.
memory.why_now: As AI moves onto specialized edge accelerators, multi-model interference and reallocation overhead become real deployment bottlenecks rather than lab artifacts. This paper makes that cost explicit and offers a scheduler that trades a bit of flexibility for much better deadline behavior.
memory.why_not_higher: The contribution is tied to autonomous driving pipelines on tile-based accelerators, so the audience is narrower than mainstream AI systems papers. The memory/storage angle is adjacent through data movement and reallocation cost, not a core cache or storage mechanism.
downgrade_reasons: Autonomous-driving-specific workload
Tile-accelerator assumptions limit transfer
Memory link is adjacent rather than core
