More scheduling framework than broad AI inflection point
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same isolation-aware scheduling ideas working on broader multi-model serving or robotics workloads, ideally on more common accelerator stacks with clear data-movement savings.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the real bottleneck in self-driving AI is not any one model, but the hidden cost of constantly reshuffling dozens of them across accelerator tiles under a 100 ms deadline?

monitor.title: SkillRL: Evolving Agents via Recursive Skill-Augmented Reinforcement Learning
monitor.abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have shown stunning results in complex tasks, yet they often operate in isolation, failing to learn from past experiences. Existing memory-based methods primarily store raw trajectories, which are often redundant and noise-heavy. This prevents agents from extracting high-level, reusable behavioral patterns that are essential for generalization. In this paper, we propose SkillRL, a framework that bridges the gap between raw experience and policy improvement through automatic skill discovery and recursive evolution. Our approach introduces an experience-based distillation mechanism to build a hierarchical skill library SkillBank, an adaptive retrieval strategy for general and task-specific heuristics, and a recursive evolution mechanism that allows the skill library to co-evolve with the agent's policy during reinforcement learning. These innovations significantly reduce the token footprint while enhancing reasoning utility. Experimental results on ALFWorld, WebShop and seven search-augmented tasks demonstrate that SkillRL achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming strong baselines over 15.3% and maintaining robustness as task complexity increases. Code is available at this https://github.com/aiming-lab/SkillRL.
monitor.why_now: Agent systems are still struggling to learn from prior runs without bloating context windows, so a paper that distills reusable skills instead of hoarding raw trajectories is timely. The token-efficiency angle also maps to a live practical pain point in LLM agent design.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory story is behavioral memory, not memory/storage systems, so it does not satisfy the podcast's storage-first lens. The evidence is also centered on agent benchmarks like ALFWorld and WebShop rather than production-grade workloads.
downgrade_reasons: Not a direct memory/storage paper
Benchmark-heavy agent evaluation
Transfer beyond task-oriented LLM agents is still uncertain
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger case on open-ended real agent workflows, with clearer ablations and evidence that the skill library transfers across domains and model families, would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if AI agents stopped stockpiling noisy transcripts and instead compiled experience into reusable skills that keep improving the policy over time?

monitor.title: From Assistant to Double Agent: Formalizing and Benchmarking Attacks on OpenClaw for Personalized Local AI Agent
monitor.abstract: Although large language model (LLM)-based agents, exemplified by OpenClaw, are increasingly evolving from task-oriented systems into personalized AI assistants for solving complex real-world tasks, their practical deployment also introduces severe security risks. However, existing agent security research and evaluation frameworks primarily focus on synthetic or task-centric settings, and thus fail to accurately capture the attack surface and risk propagation mechanisms of personalized agents in real-world deployments. To address this gap, we propose Personalized Agent Security Bench (PASB), an end-to-end security evaluation framework tailored for real-world personalized agents. Building upon existing agent attack paradigms, PASB incorporates personalized usage scenarios, realistic toolchains, and long-horizon interactions, enabling black-box, end-to-end security evaluation on real systems. Using OpenClaw as a representative case study, we systematically evaluate its security across multiple personalized scenarios, tool capabilities, and attack types. Our results indicate that OpenClaw exhibits critical vulnerabilities at different execution stages, including user prompt processing, tool usage, and memory retrieval, highlighting substantial security risks in personalized agent deployments. The code for the proposed PASB framework is available at https://github.com/AstorYH/PASB.
monitor.why_now: Personalized local agents are moving from demos into real use, and this paper tests how they fail under long-horizon, tool-using, black-box attacks instead of toy prompt-injection setups. That makes it timely for people deploying agentic assistants.
monitor.why_not_higher: The main contribution is still a benchmark and an OpenClaw case study, not a defense or architecture shift that the wider field will immediately adopt. Its memory angle is about agent memory retrieval as an attack surface, not memory/storage systems in the podcast's core sense.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage link is incidental rather than systems-level
Evidence is centered on OpenClaw rather than multiple agent stacks
More evaluation benchmark than practice-changing method
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show the framework and attack patterns transferring across several widely used agent platforms, with mitigations that materially reduce failures.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: If your local AI assistant has tools, memory, and autonomy, how do you stop it from becoming a double agent?

monitor.title: HumanLM: Simulating Users with State Alignment Beats Response Imitation
monitor.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used to simulate how specific users respond to a given context, enabling more user-centric applications that rely on user feedback. However, existing user simulators mostly imitate surface-level patterns and language styles, which fail to reflect the underlying states of real users (e.g., beliefs and emotions). To address these limitations, we propose a novel training framework, HumanLM, which builds user simulators that accurately reflect real users. Our key insight is that, in addition to generating responses, the model should generate natural-language latent states that align with ground-truth responses through reinforcement learning. These latent states correspond to a set of psychologically grounded state dimensions that drive how real users respond. HumanLM further synthesizes these aligned latent states into responses that accurately represent real users. For extensive evaluation, we develop Humanual, a comprehensive benchmark for simulating real users based on public data. Humanual consists of six large-scale datasets with 26k users and 216k responses in total, spanning diverse tasks such as generating user responses to daily life issues, political blogs, and chat sessions with LLM assistants. Across datasets, HumanLM significantly outperforms alternative approaches, achieving an average relative improvement of 16.3% in alignment scores from an LLM judge. In a real-time simulation study with 111 participants, HumanLM achieves the highest similarity to real user responses and competitive human-likeness scores.
monitor.why_now: LLM products increasingly rely on synthetic users for evaluation, personalization, and feedback loops, so a paper arguing that internal state alignment beats surface imitation is timely. The new Humanual benchmark and live-participant study make this more than a pure prompt-engineering tweak.
monitor.why_not_higher: This is still mainly a user-simulation paper, not a broader shift in how mainstream AI systems are built or served. The strongest gains are benchmark and judge-alignment wins rather than clear downstream product or systems improvements.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection is absent
User simulation is still a niche concern for much of the broader AI audience
Evidence leans heavily on alignment-style evaluation rather than deployed impact
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show that state-aligned simulators materially improve real agent evaluation, personalization, or product decisions in deployed LLM systems.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the missing ingredient in AI user simulation is not better mimicry of people's words, but modeling the hidden beliefs and emotions that produce them?

monitor.title: Scaling Reasoning Efficiently via Relaxed On-Policy Distillation
monitor.abstract: On-policy distillation is pivotal for transferring reasoning capabilities to capacity-constrained models, yet remains prone to instability and negative transfer. We show that on-policy distillation can be interpreted, both theoretically and empirically, as a form of policy optimization, where the teacher-student log-likelihood ratio acts as a token reward. From this insight, we introduce REOPOLD (Relaxed On-Policy Distillation) a framework that stabilizes optimization by relaxing the strict imitation constraints of standard on-policy distillation. Specifically, REOPOLD temperately and selectively leverages rewards from the teacher through mixture-based reward clipping, entropy-based token-level dynamic sampling, and a unified exploration-to-refinement training strategy. Empirically, REOPOLD surpasses its baselines with superior sample efficiency during training and enhanced test-time scaling at inference, across mathematical, visual, and agentic tool-use reasoning tasks. Specifically, REOPOLD outperforms recent RL approaches achieving 6.7~12x greater sample efficiency and enables a 7B student to match a 32B teacher in visual reasoning with a ~3.32x inference speedup.
monitor.why_now: Reasoning post-training is one of the most active areas in AI, and a method that claims RL-level gains with much better sample efficiency will get attention quickly. The promise of pushing smaller models closer to larger teachers also fits the current demand for cheaper reasoning systems.
monitor.why_not_higher: The paper looks more like a strong training recipe than a clear step-change in deployed systems practice, and the abstract does not show much hardware or serving realism. It also has little direct connection to memory or storage mechanisms, which limits its fit for the second editorial lens.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage link is not central
Crowded reasoning distillation and RL efficiency area
Limited deployment-scale or hardware-specific evidence in the abstract
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A convincing open-model reproduction showing this recipe reliably beats standard RL or strict on-policy distillation across widely used reasoning benchmarks would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a 7B model learn to reason like a 32B teacher without paying the full RL training bill?

monitor.title: Demystifying OPD: Length Inflation and Stabilization Strategies for Large Language Models
monitor.abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) trains student models under their own induced distribution while leveraging supervision from stronger teachers. We identify a failure mode of OPD: as training progresses, on-policy rollouts can undergo abrupt length inflation, causing truncated trajectories to dominate the training data. This truncation collapse coincides with abrupt repetition saturation and induces biased gradient signals, leading to severe training instability and sharp degradation in validation performance. We attribute this problem to the interaction between student-induced data collection and the distillation objective, which implicitly favors long and repetitive rollouts. To address this issue, we propose StableOPD, a stabilized OPD framework that combines a reference-based divergence constraint with rollout mixture distillation. These together mitigate repetition-induced length inflation and further stabilize OPD training. Across multiple math reasoning datasets, our approach prevents truncation collapse, stabilizes training dynamics, and improves performance by 7.2% on average.
monitor.why_now: As teams push smaller reasoning models through post-training and distillation, instability in on-policy rollouts becomes a practical bottleneck. A paper that identifies and mitigates a specific collapse mode in OPD is timely for people trying to make reasoning distillation reliable.
monitor.why_not_higher: The contribution is mainly a stabilization recipe for a specific training regime rather than a broad shift in how most teams train or serve LLMs. The memory/storage angle is effectively absent, and the evaluation appears concentrated on math reasoning settings rather than wider production-style workloads.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection is absent
Impact is concentrated in on-policy distillation workflows
Evaluation is narrow to reasoning benchmarks
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the same failure mode and fix matter across larger models, more diverse tasks, and mainstream post-training pipelines would raise priority.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why do distilled reasoning models suddenly start talking longer, repeating themselves, and then collapsing in training, and what does that reveal about the hidden incentives inside on-policy distillation?

monitor.title: Dr. MAS: Stable Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Agent LLM Systems
monitor.abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems enable advanced reasoning and tool use via role specialization, yet reliable reinforcement learning (RL) post-training for such systems remains difficult. In this work, we theoretically pinpoint a key reason for training instability when extending group-based RL to multi-agent LLM systems. We show that under GRPO-style optimization, a global normalization baseline may deviate from diverse agents' reward distributions, which ultimately leads to gradient-norm instability. Based on this finding, we propose Dr. MAS, a simple and stable RL training recipe for multi-agent LLM systems. Dr. MAS uses an agent-wise remedy: normalizing advantages per agent using each agent's own reward statistics, which calibrates gradient scales and dramatically stabilizes training, both theoretically and empirically. Beyond the algorithm, Dr. MAS provides an end-to-end RL training framework for multi-agent LLM systems, supporting scalable orchestration, flexible per-agent LLM serving and optimization configs, and shared resource scheduling of LLM actor backends. We evaluate Dr. MAS on multi-agent math reasoning and multi-turn search benchmarks using Qwen2.5 and Qwen3 series models. Dr. MAS achieves clear gains over vanilla GRPO (e.g., +5.6\% avg@16 and +4.6\% pass@16 on math, and +15.2\% avg@16 and +13.1\% pass@16 on search) while largely eliminating gradient spikes. Moreover, it remains highly effective under heterogeneous agent-model assignments while improving efficiency.
monitor.why_now: Multi-agent LLM systems are getting real attention, and this paper targets a concrete failure mode in RL post-training rather than just adding another agent benchmark. The per-agent normalization fix is simple enough to matter if teams start training specialized agent ensembles more seriously.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage link is effectively absent, so it does not earn the podcast's second lens. It is also still a niche training recipe for multi-agent RL, with evidence centered on math and search rather than broader deployment settings.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage mechanism is not central
Multi-agent RL post-training is still a narrow workflow
Validation is concentrated on benchmark-style agent tasks
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger evidence on broader agent workloads and clear uptake in practical multi-agent training stacks would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why do multi-agent LLMs become unstable under GRPO, and can a simple agent-by-agent normalization trick make RL for agent teams actually usable?

monitor.title: Mousse: Rectifying the Geometry of Muon with Curvature-Aware Preconditioning
monitor.abstract: Recent advances in spectral optimization, notably Muon, have demonstrated that constraining update steps to the Stiefel manifold can significantly accelerate training and improve generalization. However, Muon implicitly assumes an isotropic optimization landscape, enforcing a uniform spectral update norm across all eigen-directions. We argue that this "egalitarian" constraint is suboptimal for Deep Neural Networks, where the curvature spectrum is known to be highly heavy-tailed and ill-conditioned. In such landscapes, Muon risks amplifying instabilities in high-curvature directions while limiting necessary progress in flat directions. In this work, we propose \textbf{Mousse} (\textbf{M}uon \textbf{O}ptimization \textbf{U}tilizing \textbf{S}hampoo's \textbf{S}tructural \textbf{E}stimation), a novel optimizer that reconciles the structural stability of spectral methods with the geometric adaptivity of second-order preconditioning. Instead of applying Newton-Schulz orthogonalization directly to the momentum matrix, Mousse operates in a whitened coordinate system induced by Kronecker-factored statistics (derived from Shampoo). Mathematically, we formulate Mousse as the solution to a spectral steepest descent problem constrained by an anisotropic trust region, where the optimal update is derived via the polar decomposition of the whitened gradient. Empirical results across language models ranging from 160M to 800M parameters demonstrate that Mousse consistently outperforms Muon, achieving around $\sim$12\% reduction in training steps with negligible computational overhead.
monitor.why_now: Training efficiency is a live topic, and Muon-style spectral optimizers have enough mindshare that a curvature-aware fix is timely. If the reported ~12% step reduction holds up, this could matter for teams tuning mid-scale LLM training runs now.
monitor.why_not_higher: This is still mainly an optimizer refinement rather than a clear change to serving or memory systems, and the evidence only reaches 800M-parameter models. That makes it interesting to watch, but not an obvious must-cover episode yet.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Evidence stops below frontier-scale training
More optimizer refinement than platform-shifting systems change
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show consistent wins on multi-billion-parameter runs with strong baseline comparisons and clear adoption or cost evidence.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if Muon's core mistake is treating every training direction equally, and a curvature-aware remix with Shampoo can cut LLM training steps by 12%?

monitor.title: Loop, Think, & Generalize: Implicit Reasoning in Recurrent-Depth Transformers
monitor.abstract: We study implicit reasoning, i.e. the ability to combine knowledge or rules within a single forward pass. While transformer-based large language models store substantial factual knowledge and rules, they often fail to compose this knowledge for implicit multi-hop reasoning, suggesting a lack of compositional generalization over their parametric knowledge. To address this limitation, we study recurrent-depth transformers, which enables iterative computation over the same transformer layers. We investigate two compositional generalization challenges under the implicit reasoning scenario: systematic generalization, i.e. combining knowledge that is never used for compositions during training, and depth extrapolation, i.e. generalizing from limited reasoning depth (e.g. training on up to 5-hop) to deeper compositions (e.g. 10-hop). Through controlled studies with models trained from scratch, we show that while vanilla transformers struggle with both generalization challenges, recurrent-depth transformers can effectively make such generalization. For systematic generalization, we find that this ability emerges through a three-stage grokking process, transitioning from memorization to in-distribution generalization and finally to systematic generalization, supported by mechanistic analysis. For depth extrapolation, we show that generalization beyond training depth can be unlocked by scaling inference-time recurrence, with more iterations enabling deeper reasoning. We further study how training strategies affect extrapolation, providing guidance on training recurrent-depth transformers, and identify a key limitation, overthinking, where excessive recurrence degrades predictions and limits generalization to very deep compositions.
monitor.why_now: Reasoning and inference-time compute are active topics, and this paper offers a clean claim that recurrence can improve compositional reasoning and depth extrapolation where vanilla transformers fail. The identified overthinking failure mode also gives it a timely angle for current discussions about scaling test-time computation.
monitor.why_not_higher: The evidence is mainly from controlled from-scratch studies rather than realistic frontier-scale models, production workloads, or serving settings, so the practical payoff is still uncertain. It also does not have a direct memory/storage mechanism or systems contribution.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage link is absent rather than central
Evaluation appears controlled and synthetic rather than deployment-realistic
Immediate impact on how people train or serve modern LLMs is still speculative
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A strong result on capable open LLMs or realistic reasoning benchmarks showing recurrent-depth beats standard test-time compute baselines at acceptable cost would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if better reasoning comes not from a bigger transformer, but from letting the same transformer loop over its own thoughts until it generalizes?

monitor.title: Explainable LLM Unlearning Through Reasoning
monitor.abstract: LLM unlearning is essential for mitigating safety, copyright, and privacy concerns in pre-trained large language models (LLMs). Compared to preference alignment, it offers a more explicit way by removing undesirable knowledge characterized by specific unlearning datasets. In previous works, gradient ascent (GA) and its variants have shown promise for implementing unlearning, yet their untargeted nature results in unintended degradation of general capabilities, incomplete removal of knowledge, and the generation of incoherent responses, among many others. We argue that these issues stem from the absence of explicit guidance on what and how models should unlearn. To fill this gap, we introduce a novel unlearning target, reasoning-based unlearning target, which satisfies both the specified unlearning scope and the specified post-unlearning response. Building on this, we propose targeted reasoning unlearning (TRU), which leverages reasoning-based unlearning target as guidance. We employ the target using a cross-entropy supervised loss combined with a GA-based loss, enabling the model to learn reasoning ability for precise knowledge removal while preserving unrelated abilities. We evaluate TRU against strong baselines across multiple benchmarks and LLM backbones, and find that it achieves more reliable unlearning while preserving general capabilities. Moreover, TRU exhibits superior robustness under diverse attack scenarios, stemming from the reasoning ability learned through reasoning-based targets. Overall, our study establishes reasoning-augmented unlearning as a practical paradigm for reliable and explainable LLM unlearning.
monitor.why_now: Unlearning is moving from a policy talking point to an operational requirement for safety, copyright, and privacy takedowns, so methods that promise cleaner deletion without wrecking general ability are timely. The core idea here, telling the model how to forget rather than only pushing it away with gradient ascent, is relevant to current post-training practice.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage lens is absent, and the abstract does not establish frontier-scale realism or production-style deletion workloads. It may land as a strong alignment benchmark result more than a clear change in how most teams will run LLM unlearning soon.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Real-world deployment scale is unclear
May be more benchmark progress than workflow-changing practice
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence on larger production-relevant models and real takedown or privacy deletion workflows with clear utility preservation under attack would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can we teach a model to forget specific knowledge without making it worse at everything else, and is reasoning the missing control knob?

monitor.title: Crosslingual On-Policy Self-Distillation for Multilingual Reasoning
monitor.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in mathematical reasoning, but this ability is not equally accessible across languages. Especially low-resource languages exhibit much lower reasoning performance. To address this, we propose Crosslingual On-Policy Self-Distillation (COPSD), which transfers a model's own high-resource reasoning behavior to low-resource languages. COPSD uses the same model as student and teacher: the student sees only the low-resource problem, while the teacher receives privileged crosslingual context, including the problem translation and reference solution in English. Training minimizes full-distribution token-level divergence on the student's own rollouts, providing dense supervision while avoiding the sparsity and instability of outcome-only reinforcement learning (RL). Experiments on 17 low-resource African languages show that COPSD consistently improves low-resource mathematical reasoning across model sizes and substantially outperforms Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Further analyses show that COPSD improves answer-format adherence, strengthens test-time scaling, and generalizes to harder multilingual reasoning benchmarks, with especially large gains for lower-resource languages. We make our code and data available at: https://github.com/cisnlp/COPSD.
monitor.why_now: Reasoning gains are still heavily concentrated in English and other high-resource languages, so a method that lifts low-resource multilingual reasoning is timely. The paper also taps into a live question in AI training: whether dense self-distillation can beat brittle RL-style optimization for reasoning.
monitor.why_not_higher: This is still mostly a multilingual math-reasoning training paper rather than a result that clearly changes mainstream model building or serving. It also has no direct memory or storage mechanism, so it does not fit the second editorial lens.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage connection
Impact is concentrated in multilingual reasoning benchmarks
Unclear whether the method will transfer into common production LLM workflows
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same recipe improving broader multilingual assistant tasks on widely used model families, not just math-reasoning benchmarks.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a model teach itself to reason in low-resource languages by borrowing its own stronger English-side supervision, without the instability of RL?

monitor.title: KernelSkill: A Multi-Agent Framework for GPU Kernel Optimization
monitor.abstract: Improving GPU kernel efficiency is crucial for advancing AI systems. Recent work has explored leveraging large language models (LLMs) for GPU kernel generation and optimization. However, existing LLM-based kernel optimization pipelines typically rely on opaque, implicitly learned heuristics within the LLMs to determine optimization strategies. This leads to inefficient trial-and-error and weakly interpretable optimizations. Our key insight is to replace implicit heuristics with expert optimization skills that are knowledge-driven and aware of task trajectories. Specifically, we present KernelSkill, a multi-agent framework with a dual-level memory architecture. KernelSkill operates by coordinating agents with long-term memory of reusable expert skills and short-term memory to prevent repetitive backtracking. On KernelBench Levels 1-3, KernelSkill achieves a 100% success rate and average speedups of 5.44x, 2.82x, and 1.92x over Torch Eager on Levels 1, 2, and 3, respectively, outperforming prior baselines. Code is available at https://github.com/0satan0/KernelMem/.
monitor.why_now: LLM-assisted coding is moving from toy generation demos toward real systems optimization, and GPU kernels remain a stubborn throughput bottleneck for AI workloads. A framework that claims reusable optimization skills rather than opaque trial-and-error is timely for teams watching agentic tooling mature.
monitor.why_not_higher: The paper looks more like a strong benchmark-and-tooling result than a clear shift in how most teams build or serve AI systems today. Its memory language is about agent workflow memory, not the podcast's memory/storage lens, and the abstract does not show end-to-end gains on production model pipelines.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage link is indirect rather than central
Evidence is benchmark-centric rather than end-to-end
Transfer to real training and serving stacks is still unclear
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show durable wins on widely used production kernels or full model training and inference pipelines, with evidence that the optimization skills transfer beyond KernelBench.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a team of specialist LLM agents turn GPU kernel tuning from blind trial-and-error into a reusable optimization playbook?

monitor.title: ARO: A New Lens On Matrix Optimization For Large Models
monitor.abstract: Matrix-based optimizers have attracted growing interest for improving LLM training efficiency, with significant progress centered on orthogonalization/whitening based methods. While yielding substantial performance gains, a fundamental question arises: can we develop new paradigms beyond orthogonalization, pushing the efficiency frontier further? We present \textbf{Adaptively Rotated Optimization (ARO}, a new matrix optimization framework that treats gradient rotation as a first class design principle. ARO accelerates LLM training by performing normed steepest descent in a rotated coordinate system, where the rotation is determined by a novel norm-informed policy. This perspective yields update rules that go beyond existing orthogonalization and whitening optimizers, improving sample efficiency in practice. To make comparisons reliable, we propose a rigorously controlled benchmarking protocol that reduces confounding and bias. Under this protocol, ARO consistently outperforms AdamW (by 1.3 $\sim$1.35$\times$) and orthogonalization methods (by 1.1$\sim$1.15$\times$) in LLM pretraining at up to 8B activated parameters, and up to $8\times$ overtrain budget, without evidence of diminishing returns. Finally, we discuss how ARO can be reformulated as a symmetry-aware optimizer grounded in rotational symmetries of residual streams, motivating advanced designs that enable computationally efficient exploitation of cross-layer/cross module couplings.
monitor.why_now: LLM pretraining is still expensive enough that a credible optimizer beating AdamW and recent orthogonalization methods would matter quickly to labs chasing better sample efficiency. The paper also pitches a fresh optimizer design lens rather than just another small Adam variant.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage tie is effectively absent, and optimizer papers often look better in controlled benchmarks than in messy production training stacks. The evidence is promising but still below true frontier-scale training, with overhead and robustness questions left open by the abstract.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Optimizer adoption risk is high
Results stop short of frontier-scale runs
Runtime overhead and stability tradeoffs are unclear
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Independent reproduction or larger-scale training results showing the gains persist without costly optimizer overhead would raise priority.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Is ARO the first real post-AdamW training idea for large models, or just the latest optimizer win that disappears at scale?

monitor.title: Benchmarking Empirical Privacy Protection for Adaptations of Large Language Models
monitor.abstract: Recent work has applied differential privacy (DP) to adapt large language models (LLMs) for sensitive applications, offering theoretical guarantees. However, its practical effectiveness remains unclear, partly due to LLM pretraining, where overlaps and interdependencies with adaptation data can undermine privacy despite DP efforts. To analyze this issue in practice, we investigate privacy risks under DP adaptations in LLMs using state-of-the-art attacks such as robust membership inference and canary data extraction. We benchmark these risks by systematically varying the adaptation data distribution, from exact overlaps with pretraining data, through in-distribution (IID) cases, to entirely out-of-distribution (OOD) examples. Additionally, we evaluate how different adaptation methods and different privacy regimes impact the vulnerability. Our results show that distribution shifts strongly influence privacy vulnerability: the closer the adaptation data is to the pretraining distribution, the higher the practical privacy risk at the same theoretical guarantee, even without direct data overlap. We find that parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods, such as LoRA, achieve the highest empirical privacy protection for OOD data. Our benchmark identifies key factors for achieving practical privacy in DP LLM adaptation, providing actionable insights for deploying customized models in sensitive settings. Looking forward, we propose a structured framework for holistic privacy assessment beyond adaptation privacy, to identify and evaluate risks across the full pretrain-adapt pipeline of LLMs.
monitor.why_now: Teams are pushing LLM adaptation into sensitive domains right as differential privacy is being treated as a deployment checkbox. This paper argues that pretraining-distribution effects can materially change real privacy risk even when the formal DP guarantee is held fixed.
monitor.why_not_higher: This is still a benchmark-and-evaluation paper rather than a new privacy method or a widely adopted deployment recipe. It also has no real memory/storage angle, so it only competes on the public-interest lens.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage contribution
Benchmark result more than a new method
Broad-audience urgency is moderate, not top-tier
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger push would come from large-model results on widely used adaptation stacks with clear deployment thresholds and mitigation guidance practitioners can apply immediately.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if your differentially private LLM fine-tune is less protected not because DP failed on paper, but because your data looks too much like what the model already saw before training?

monitor.title: Sparse Models, Sparse Safety: Unsafe Routes in Mixture-of-Experts LLMs
monitor.abstract: By introducing routers to selectively activate experts in Transformer layers, the mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture significantly reduces computational costs in large language models (LLMs) while maintaining competitive performance, especially for models with massive parameters. However, prior work has largely focused on utility and efficiency, leaving the safety risks associated with this sparse architecture underexplored. In this work, we show that the safety of MoE LLMs is as sparse as their architecture by discovering unsafe routes: routing configurations that, once activated, convert safe outputs into harmful ones. Specifically, we first introduce the Router Safety importance score (RoSais) to quantify the safety criticality of each layer's router. Manipulation of only the high-RoSais router(s) can flip the default route into an unsafe one. For instance, on JailbreakBench, masking 5 routers in DeepSeek-V2-Lite increases attack success rate (ASR) by over 4$\times$ to 0.79, highlighting an inherent risk that router manipulation may naturally occur in MoE LLMs. We further propose a Fine-grained token-layer-wise Stochastic Optimization framework to discover more concrete Unsafe Routes (F-SOUR), which explicitly considers the sequentiality and dynamics of input tokens. Across four representative MoE LLM families, F-SOUR achieves an average ASR of 0.90 and 0.98 on JailbreakBench and AdvBench, respectively. Finally, we outline defensive perspectives, including safety-aware route disabling and router training, as promising directions to safeguard MoE LLMs. We hope our work can inform future red-teaming and safeguarding of MoE LLMs. Our code is provided in https://github.com/TrustAIRLab/UnsafeMoE.
monitor.why_now: MoE models are moving into mainstream deployment, and this paper argues that sparsity creates safety-critical routing paths that standard evaluations can miss. It gives a concrete, timely safety angle on a major model architecture trend.
monitor.why_not_higher: This is not really a memory/storage paper, and its main contribution is a jailbreak-style evaluation result rather than a clear change to mainstream training or serving practice. The attack model is compelling but still somewhat closer to white-box route manipulation than an everyday deployment failure mode.
downgrade_reasons: Weak memory/storage relevance
Operational attack surface is not fully established
Leans heavily on benchmark attack success rates
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger real-world exploit path or demonstrated mitigation that materially changes how MoE systems are deployed would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if an MoE model's safety lives on just a handful of routing decisions, and flipping the wrong routes turns a guarded assistant into an easy jailbreak?

monitor.title: PACED: Distillation and On-Policy Self-Distillation at the Frontier of Student Competence
monitor.abstract: Standard LLM distillation treats all training problems equally -- wasting compute on problems the student has already mastered or cannot yet solve. We empirically show that this inefficiency has a precise gradient-level signature: the cross-problem gradient signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) follows a bell curve over student pass rate, collapsing at both extremes.   We propose PACED, which weights each problem by $w(p) = p(1{-}p)$ where $p$ is the student's empirical pass rate -- concentrating training on the zone of proximal development. This requires only student rollouts, no architectural changes, and no hyperparameters. We prove the Beta kernel $w(p) = p^α(1{-}p)^β$ is the leading-order optimal weight family arising from the SNR boundary-collapse structure, and is minimax-robust under misspecification (worst-case efficiency loss $O(δ^2)$).   Across Qwen3, Qwen2.5, and Llama-3 families, PACED sets a new state of the art in our experimental setting on MATH-500, AIME~2024, and AIME~2025, improving over unweighted distillation by up to $\mathbf{+8.2}$ and over the strong AKL baseline by up to $\mathbf{+3.6}$, while reducing forgetting to $\mathbf{1.4\%}$ and $\mathbf{0.6\%}$ in distillation and self-distillation. A two-stage forward-then-reverse KL schedule pushes gains further to $\mathbf{+5.8}$ over standard forward KL on the hardest benchmark.
monitor.why_now: Reasoning-model post-training and distillation are active battlegrounds, and PACED offers a very simple recipe for spending training compute where the student can still learn. The zone-of-proximal-development framing is intuitive and the reported gains are large enough to get practitioners' attention.
monitor.why_not_higher: The paper is not a memory/storage paper, and its evidence is concentrated in math and competition-style reasoning benchmarks rather than a wider set of post-training workloads. That makes the headline interesting, but not yet clearly field-shaping for the broader AI audience.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory or storage mechanism
Validation is narrow to reasoning and math benchmarks
Impact is mainly on distillation pipelines rather than the full AI systems stack
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same weighting scheme improving broader post-training workloads, model families, and real production-style evaluation settings beyond math reasoning.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the best way to train a smaller reasoning model is to stop teaching it what it already knows and focus only on the problems right at the edge of its competence?

monitor.title: Understanding Dynamic Compute Allocation in Recurrent Transformers
monitor.abstract: Token-level adaptive computation seeks to reduce inference cost by allocating more computation to harder tokens and less to easier ones. However, prior work is primarily evaluated on natural-language benchmarks using task-level metrics, where token-level difficulty is unobservable and confounded with architectural factors, making it unclear whether compute allocation truly aligns with underlying complexity. We address this gap through three contributions. First, we introduce a complexity-controlled evaluation paradigm using algorithmic and synthetic language tasks with parameterized difficulty, enabling direct testing of token-level compute allocation. Second, we propose ANIRA, a unified recurrent Transformer framework that supports per-token variable-depth computation while isolating compute allocation decisions from other model factors. Third, we use this framework to conduct a systematic analysis of token-level adaptive computation across alignment with complexity, generalization, and decision timing. Our results show that compute allocation aligned with task complexity can emerge without explicit difficulty supervision, but such alignment does not imply algorithmic generalization: models fail to extrapolate to unseen input sizes despite allocating additional computation. We further find that early compute decisions rely on static structural cues, whereas online halting more closely tracks algorithmic execution state.
monitor.why_now: Adaptive and test-time compute are active themes, and this paper gives a useful reality check on a popular intuition: spending more compute on harder tokens does not automatically buy better generalization. That makes it timely as the field revisits recurrence and variable-depth inference.
monitor.why_not_higher: The evaluation is mostly synthetic and complexity-controlled rather than on realistic LLM workloads, benchmarks, or hardware. It is stronger as a diagnostic analysis paper than as a paper that changes what teams will build or serve in the next quarter.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage link is absent
Synthetic tasks dominate the evidence
No clear serving or training recipe
Limited near-term operational impact
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A follow-up showing the same lessons or a real efficiency-quality win on modern long-context LLM inference would raise priority.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if a model learns to spend more compute on the right tokens and still fails when the problem truly gets harder?

monitor.title: ES-dLLM: Efficient Inference for Diffusion Large Language Models by Early-Skipping
monitor.abstract: Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) are emerging as a promising alternative to autoregressive models (ARMs) due to their ability to capture bidirectional context and the potential for parallel generation. Despite the advantages, dLLM inference remains computationally expensive as the full input context is processed at every iteration. In this work, we analyze the generation dynamics of dLLMs and find that intermediate representations, including key, value, and hidden states, change only subtly across successive iterations. Leveraging this insight, we propose \textbf{ES-dLLM}, a training-free inference acceleration framework for dLLM that reduces computation by skipping tokens in early layers based on the estimated importance. Token importance is computed with intermediate tensor variation and confidence scores of previous iterations. Experiments on LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B demonstrate that ES-dLLM achieves throughput of up to 226.57 and 308.51 tokens per second (TPS), respectively, on an NVIDIA H200 GPU, delivering 5.6$\times$ to 16.8$\times$ speedup over the vanilla implementation and up to 1.85$\times$ over the state-of-the-art caching method, while preserving generation quality.
monitor.why_now: Diffusion LLMs are getting real attention as an alternative to autoregressive decoding, and inference cost is the main blocker. A training-free method claiming large speedups directly attacks the practical bottleneck that determines whether dLLMs can matter beyond demos.
monitor.why_not_higher: The paper is still tied to the narrower dLLM track rather than mainstream LLM serving, so its near-term audience is limited. The memory/storage angle is indirect, and the evidence appears concentrated on a small set of models and hardware.
downgrade_reasons: dLLMs are not yet a mainstream deployment target
memory/storage relevance is adjacent rather than core
validation scope looks limited across models and hardware
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Stronger cross-model and cross-hardware replication, especially with clear quality-latency tradeoffs on broader dLLM workloads, would raise it.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: If diffusion LLMs keep revisiting almost-unchanged states, can we skip enough early work to make them genuinely practical to serve?

monitor.title: Towards Efficient Large Language Reasoning Models via Extreme-Ratio Chain-of-Thought Compression
monitor.abstract: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning successfully enhances the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet it incurs substantial computational overhead for inference. Existing CoT compression methods often suffer from a critical loss of logical fidelity at high compression ratios, resulting in significant performance degradation. To achieve high-fidelity, fast reasoning, we propose a novel EXTreme-RAtio Chain-of-Thought Compression framework, termed Extra-CoT, which aggressively reduces the token budget while preserving answer accuracy. To generate reliable, high-fidelity supervision, we first train a dedicated semantically-preserved compressor on mathematical CoT data with fine-grained annotations. An LLM is then fine-tuned on these compressed pairs via a mixed-ratio supervised fine-tuning (SFT), teaching it to follow a spectrum of compression budgets and providing a stable initialization for reinforcement learning (RL). We further propose Constrained and Hierarchical Ratio Policy Optimization (CHRPO) to explicitly incentivize question-solving ability under lower budgets by a hierarchical reward. Experiments on three mathematical reasoning benchmarks show the superiority of Extra-CoT. For example, on MATH-500 using Qwen3-1.7B, Extra-CoT achieves over 73\% token reduction with an accuracy improvement of 0.6\%, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods. Our source codes have been released at https://github.com/Mwie1024/Extra-CoT.
monitor.why_now: Reasoning models are running into token-cost and latency limits, so a paper claiming large chain-of-thought compression without hurting answers is timely. It speaks directly to the current test-time compute debate around making reasoning cheaper.
monitor.why_not_higher: The evidence is mostly math benchmarks on a small 1.7B model, not broader reasoning workloads or production serving settings. The memory angle is indirect: fewer reasoning tokens help KV/cache footprint, but this is not a memory-systems paper.
downgrade_reasons: Memory connection is adjacent rather than core
Validation is concentrated on math reasoning benchmarks
No real serving, latency, or hardware-level evidence
Generalization beyond this task family is still unclear
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show the same accuracy-cost tradeoff on larger reasoning models, broader tasks, and real inference measurements including latency and KV-cache savings.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a reasoning model think in shorthand instead of essays, cutting most of its tokens without losing the plot?

monitor.title: DIVE: Scaling Diversity in Agentic Task Synthesis for Generalizable Tool Use
monitor.abstract: Recent work synthesizes agentic tasks for post-training tool-using LLMs, yet robust generalization under shifts in tasks and toolsets remains an open challenge. We trace this brittleness to insufficient diversity in synthesized tasks. Scaling diversity is difficult because training requires tasks to remain executable and verifiable, while generalization demands coverage of diverse tool types, toolset combinations, and heterogeneous tool-use patterns. We propose DIVE, an evidence-driven recipe that inverts synthesis order, executing diverse, real-world tools first and reverse-deriving tasks strictly entailed by the resulting traces, thereby providing grounding by construction. DIVE scales structural diversity along two controllable axes, tool-pool coverage and per-task toolset variety, and an Evidence Collection--Task Derivation loop further induces rich multi-step tool-use patterns across 373 tools in five domains. Training Qwen3-8B on DIVE data (48k SFT + 3.2k RL) improves by +22 average points across 9 OOD benchmarks and outperforms the strongest 8B baseline by +68. Remarkably, controlled scaling analysis reveals that diversity scaling consistently outperforms quantity scaling for OOD generalization, even with 4x less data.
monitor.why_now: Tool-using agents are still brittle under task and toolset shifts, and this paper offers a concrete post-training recipe centered on executable diversity rather than just more synthetic data. The claim that diversity scaling beats quantity scaling is timely and actionable for teams building agent benchmarks and training sets.
monitor.why_not_higher: The contribution is still mostly about agentic tool-use post-training, not a broader change to core model training or serving. It also has no direct memory/storage angle, and the evidence is stronger on benchmark generalization than on deployed-agent impact.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Primarily an agentic tool-use training paper rather than a general AI systems paper
Transfer to real production agents is not yet fully established
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Demonstrate the same diversity-first recipe improving real deployed agents on unseen enterprise toolchains or broader frontier-model settings beyond benchmark suites.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if agentic LLMs are failing not because they need more training data, but because their synthetic tool-use experience is too narrow to generalize?

monitor.title: XPERT: Expert Knowledge Transfer for Effective Training of Language Models
monitor.abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models organize knowledge into explicitly routed expert modules, making expert-level representations traceable and analyzable. By analyzing expert activation patterns in MoE large language models (LLMs), we find that a subset of experts is consistently activated across diverse knowledge domains. These common experts encode cross-domain, generalizable knowledge that is closely related to model generalization, naturally raising the question of how such identifiable expert knowledge can be practically reused. Motivated by this observation, we propose XPERT, a framework that extracts, consolidates, and reuses expert knowledge from pre-trained MoE LLMs to support more effective training of language models across different model scales. XPERT identifies cross-domain experts via inference-only analysis, refines their representations through tensor decomposition, and adapts the extracted knowledge to reuse in downstream models. Experiments on language understanding and dialogue generation benchmarks show that models benefiting from reused expert knowledge achieve consistently stronger performance and faster convergence compared to strong baselines. These results highlight MoE LLMs as structured and reusable knowledge sources, and demonstrate the value of expert-level knowledge reuse for improving model training.
monitor.why_now: As MoE LLMs become more common, a method for extracting reusable cross-domain expert knowledge is timely because it points to cheaper and more structured ways to improve training. The paper also taps into a live question about what sparse experts actually store and how portable that knowledge is.
monitor.why_not_higher: The abstract still reads mostly like a training-method benchmark result rather than a clear change in how most teams will build models in the next cycle. It also does not have a direct memory or storage mechanism, so it is weak on the podcast's second lens.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection is absent
Evidence appears benchmark-centric rather than deployment-scale
Adoption path for mainstream training stacks is still unclear
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show strong gains on large modern MoE checkpoints and a convincing transfer recipe that holds up in production-scale training settings.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the most reusable part of a giant MoE model is not the full checkpoint, but a small set of experts carrying its most general knowledge?

deferred.title: Defeat the Heap: Zero-Copy Data Movement in AXI4MLIR
deferred.abstract: As custom hardware accelerators become increasingly central to machine learning workloads, efficient data transfer is critical for maximizing accelerator performance on linear algebra kernels. AXI4MLIR, an extension of the Multi-Level Intermediate Representation (MLIR) compiler framework for automated generation of host-accelerator driver code, incurs significant runtime overhead due to non-zero-copy CPU-accelerator data movement. During transfers from the host to the accelerator, data is copied from heap-allocated memory buffers into contiguous Direct Memory Access (DMA)-mapped buffers. This work identifies this copy as a redundant staging operation and eliminates it through zero-copy data movement. The optimization extends accel, an MLIR dialect introduced by AXI4MLIR, and implements lowering support that allocates buffers directly within DMA-mapped memory, thereby omitting the staging copy. We evaluate the proposed scheme using a configurable matrix-matrix multiplication accelerator and show that the zero-copy optimization reduces main memory data movement by up to 2x, increasing overall accelerator utilization.
deferred.why_now: As AI work pushes harder on custom accelerators, host-to-device data movement is becoming a more visible bottleneck, and zero-copy is a concrete systems lever. This paper isolates a wasteful staging copy in the toolchain and shows a clean bandwidth/utilization win.
deferred.why_not_higher: The result is demonstrated in a specific AXI4MLIR flow with a configurable matrix-multiplication accelerator rather than on end-to-end modern AI workloads. That makes it feel more like a solid compiler-runtime optimization than a paper that will change mainstream AI practice soon.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow AXI4MLIR-specific optimization
Evaluation is limited to accelerator-kernel benchmarks rather than real model pipelines
Weak immediate relevance to mainstream GPU training and serving stacks
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show end-to-end gains on real LLM or diffusion training/inference workloads across more common accelerator software stacks.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if a big chunk of your accelerator's memory traffic is not model work at all, but a pointless heap-to-DMA copy the compiler can eliminate?

deferred.title: SPARD: Self-Paced Curriculum for RL Alignment via Integrating Reward Dynamics and Data Utility
deferred.abstract: The evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) is shifting the focus from single, verifiable tasks toward complex, open-ended real-world scenarios, imposing significant challenges on the post-training phase. In these settings, the scale and complexity of reward systems have grown significantly, transitioning toward multi-objective formulations that encompass a comprehensive spectrum of model capabilities and application contexts. However, traditional methods typically rely on fixed reward weights, ignoring non-stationary learning dynamics and struggling with data heterogeneity across dimensions. To address these issues, we propose SPARD, a framework that establishes an automated, self-paced curriculum by perceiving learning progress to dynamically adjust multi-objective reward weights and data importance, thereby synchronizing learning intent with data utility for optimal performance. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that SPARD significantly enhances model capabilities across all domains.
deferred.why_now: Post-training is moving toward messy multi-objective alignment settings, so methods that adapt reward weighting and sample utility during RL are timely. If the effect is real, this is the kind of recipe paper labs could test in current LLM alignment stacks.
deferred.why_not_higher: The abstract reads like a training-policy optimization result in a very crowded area, with no concrete sign yet that it changes mainstream practice. It also has no direct memory/storage angle and gives little detail on model scale, deployment realism, or systems impact.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory/storage mechanism
Crowded RL/alignment optimization category
Evidence is abstract-level and benchmark-centric
Unclear impact on real production training pipelines
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: A stronger case would be frontier-scale post-training results showing robust gains across diverse reward mixes with clear operational benefits over fixed-weight baselines.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if LLM alignment worked better when the trainer stopped freezing reward weights and let the model's own learning dynamics decide what to emphasize next?

deferred.title: Linearization Explains Fine-Tuning in Large Language Models
deferred.abstract: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) is a popular class of techniques that strive to adapt large models in a scalable and resource-efficient manner. Yet, the mechanisms underlying their training performance and generalization remain underexplored. In this paper, we provide several insights into such fine-tuning through the lens of linearization. Fine-tuned models are often implicitly encouraged to remain close to the pretrained model. By making this explicit, using an Euclidean distance inductive bias in parameter space, we show that fine-tuning dynamics become equivalent to learning with the positive-definite neural tangent kernel (NTK). We specifically analyze how close the fully linear and the linearized fine-tuning optimizations are, based on the strength of the regularization. This allows us to be pragmatic about how good a model linearization is when fine-tuning large language models (LLMs). When linearization is a good model, our findings reveal a strong correlation between the eigenvalue spectrum of the NTK and the performance of model adaptation. Motivated by this, we give spectral perturbation bounds on the NTK induced by the choice of layers selected for fine-tuning. We empirically validate our theory on Low Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on LLMs. These insights not only characterize fine-tuning but also have the potential to enhance PEFT techniques, paving the way to better informed and more nimble adaptation in LLMs.
deferred.why_now: PEFT and LoRA remain central for adapting LLMs under tight cost constraints, so a paper that explains when fine-tuning behaves like NTK learning is timely. The spectral view of layer selection could eventually turn into better rules for choosing what to tune.
deferred.why_not_higher: This is still a theory-first paper with limited immediate recipe changes for practitioners, and the abstract does not show broad validation on production-scale tuning settings. Its memory relevance is effectively absent beyond PEFT's generic efficiency story.
downgrade_reasons: Weak memory/storage relevance
Theory-heavy with limited near-term builder impact
Empirical validation appears narrower than the breadth of the claims
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show a simple, reproducible layer-selection or regularization rule that improves real LoRA fine-tuning outcomes across several current LLMs and tasks at meaningful scale.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: If LoRA works because LLM fine-tuning stays surprisingly linear, can the NTK spectrum tell us ahead of time which layers are worth adapting?

deferred.title: GLM-OCR Technical Report
deferred.abstract: GLM-OCR is an efficient 0.9B-parameter compact multimodal model designed for real-world document understanding. It combines a 0.4B-parameter CogViT visual encoder with a 0.5B-parameter GLM language decoder, achieving a strong balance between computational efficiency and recognition performance. To address the inefficiency of standard autoregressive decoding in deterministic OCR tasks, GLM-OCR introduces a Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) mechanism that predicts multiple tokens per step, significantly improving decoding throughput while keeping memory overhead low through shared parameters. At the system level, a two-stage pipeline is adopted: PP-DocLayout-V3 first performs layout analysis, followed by parallel region-level recognition. Extensive evaluations on public benchmarks and industrial scenarios show that GLM-OCR achieves competitive or state-of-the-art performance in document parsing, text and formula transcription, table structure recovery, and key information extraction. Its compact architecture and structured generation make it suitable for both resource-constrained edge deployment and large-scale production systems.
deferred.why_now: Document AI is shifting from generic multimodal demos to production OCR systems where throughput, structured output, and edge deployment matter. This report is timely because it pairs a compact VLM with multi-token decoding aimed at lowering latency and cost in real document pipelines.
deferred.why_not_higher: The efficiency story is relevant, but the memory/storage link is adjacent rather than central, and the contribution stays tightly tied to OCR and document parsing. For a broad AI audience, it reads more like a strong task-focused technical report than a paper that changes mainstream AI system design.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow document-OCR scope
Memory angle is adjacent, not core
Limited evidence of transfer beyond OCR pipelines
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show that its multi-token structured decoding and compact multimodal design transfer cleanly to broader multimodal or structured-generation workloads beyond OCR.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the most useful multimodal model is not a giant generalist, but a compact OCR system that wins by decoding multiple tokens at once?

deferred.title: Deep Randomized Distributed Function Computation (DeepRDFC): Neural Distributed Channel Simulation
deferred.abstract: The randomized distributed function computation (RDFC) framework, which unifies many cutting-edge distributed computation and learning applications, is considered. An autoencoder (AE) architecture is proposed to minimize the total variation distance between the probability distribution simulated by the AE outputs and an unknown target distribution, using only data samples. We illustrate significantly high RDFC performance with communication load gains from our AEs compared to data compression methods. Our designs establish deep learning-based RDFC methods and aim to facilitate the use of RDFC methods, especially when the amount of common randomness is limited and strong function computation guarantees are required.
deferred.why_now: As distributed AI runs into bandwidth limits, learned communication schemes are a live research direction, and this paper tries to replace hand-designed channel simulation with neural methods. The angle is timely, but mostly for specialists in communication-efficient distributed learning rather than the broader AI field.
deferred.why_not_higher: The abstract does not show a concrete path to modern model training or serving stacks, and the memory angle is about abstract communication load rather than KV/cache/storage behavior. Evidence also appears benchmark-level rather than realistic large-model or hardware-backed validation.
downgrade_reasons: niche information-theory framing
weak direct relevance to modern AI systems
memory link is bandwidth-adjacent, not storage-core
realism of workloads/hardware unclear
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Show clear gains on real distributed LLM or multimodal training/inference workloads with concrete bandwidth, latency, or memory-traffic reductions.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can neural autoencoders learn the communication protocol itself, turning a dense information-theory problem into a practical recipe for bandwidth-starved distributed AI?

deferred.title: Attention-based graph neural networks: a survey
deferred.abstract: Graph neural networks (GNNs) aim to learn well-trained representations in a lower-dimension space for downstream tasks while preserving the topological structures. In recent years, attention mechanism, which is brilliant in the fields of natural language processing and computer vision, is introduced to GNNs to adaptively select the discriminative features and automatically filter the noisy information. To the best of our knowledge, due to the fast-paced advances in this domain, a systematic overview of attention-based GNNs is still missing. To fill this gap, this paper aims to provide a comprehensive survey on recent advances in attention-based GNNs. Firstly, we propose a novel two-level taxonomy for attention-based GNNs from the perspective of development history and architectural perspectives. Specifically, the upper level reveals the three developmental stages of attention-based GNNs, including graph recurrent attention networks, graph attention networks, and graph transformers. The lower level focuses on various typical architectures of each stage. Secondly, we review these attention-based methods following the proposed taxonomy in detail and summarize the advantages and disadvantages of various models. A model characteristics table is also provided for a more comprehensive comparison. Thirdly, we share our thoughts on some open issues and future directions of attention-based GNNs. We hope this survey will provide researchers with an up-to-date reference regarding applications of attention-based GNNs. In addition, to cope with the rapid development in this field, we intend to share the relevant latest papers as an open resource at https://github.com/sunxiaobei/awesome-attention-based-gnns.
deferred.why_now: This is a useful map of the attention-based GNN literature, especially for readers trying to place graph transformers in historical context. The taxonomy and curated reading list make it a solid orientation piece for the subfield.
deferred.why_not_higher: It is a survey rather than a new technical result, so it does not materially change how modern AI systems are built, trained, or served. The audience is mostly graph-learning specialists, and the memory/storage connection is absent.
downgrade_reasons: survey paper rather than a new result
niche to graph learning rather than a broad AI conversation driver
no direct memory, storage, KV-cache, or systems mechanism
limited near-term impact on mainstream AI practice
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Clear evidence that these graph-transformer ideas are transferring into mainstream foundation-model training or inference workflows would raise priority.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Graph attention evolved from early GNN modules into graph transformers, but which ideas actually mattered and why did so few of them cross into mainstream AI systems?

deferred.title: CONTRA: Conformal Prediction Region via Normalizing Flow Transformation
deferred.abstract: Density estimation and reliable prediction regions for outputs are crucial in supervised and unsupervised learning. While conformal prediction effectively generates coverage-guaranteed regions, it struggles with multi-dimensional outputs due to reliance on one-dimensional nonconformity scores. To address this, we introduce CONTRA: CONformal prediction region via normalizing flow TRAnsformation. CONTRA utilizes the latent spaces of normalizing flows to define nonconformity scores based on distances from the center. This allows for the mapping of high-density regions in latent space to sharp prediction regions in the output space, surpassing traditional hyperrectangular or elliptical conformal regions. Further, for scenarios where other predictive models are favored over flow-based models, we extend CONTRA to enhance any such model with a reliable prediction region by training a simple normalizing flow on the residuals. We demonstrate that both CONTRA and its extension maintain guaranteed coverage probability and outperform existing methods in generating accurate prediction regions across various datasets. We conclude that CONTRA is an effective tool for (conditional) density estimation, addressing the under-explored challenge of delivering multi-dimensional prediction regions.
deferred.why_now: Reliable multi-output uncertainty sets are still a real gap in applied ML, and using normalizing flows to turn conformal regions into sharper shapes is a neat methodological move. It matters most to people working on uncertainty estimation, safety-critical prediction, or density modeling rather than the broader AI field.
deferred.why_not_higher: The paper is not directly about modern AI system design, serving, or model memory behavior, so it misses the podcast's memory/storage lens. Its likely impact is mainly methodological and evaluation-oriented, not a clear shift in how most people build or deploy current large-model systems.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection is absent
Uncertainty-quantification niche is narrower than broad AI audience interest
No clear large-model, systems, or hardware validation in the abstract
Feels more like a solid stat.ML method paper than a strong episode driver
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that CONTRA materially improves uncertainty estimation for large multimodal or generative AI systems in realistic deployment settings would raise it.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can conformal prediction escape boxy uncertainty sets by warping the problem through a normalizing flow and still keep its coverage guarantees?

out_of_scope.title: 6G-Bench: An Open Benchmark for Semantic Communication and Network-Level Reasoning with Foundation Models in AI-Native 6G Networks
out_of_scope.abstract: This paper introduces 6G-Bench, an open benchmark for evaluating semantic communication and network-level reasoning in AI-native 6G networks. 6G-Bench defines a taxonomy of 30 decision-making tasks (T1--T30) extracted from ongoing 6G and AI-agent standardization activities in 3GPP, IETF, ETSI, ITU-T, and the O-RAN Alliance, and organizes them into five standardization-aligned capability categories. Starting from 113,475 scenarios, we generate a balanced pool of 10,000 very-hard multiple-choice questions using task-conditioned prompts that enforce multi-step quantitative reasoning under uncertainty and worst-case regret minimization over multi-turn horizons. After automated filtering and expert human validation, 3,722 questions are retained as a high-confidence evaluation set, while the full pool is released to support training and fine-tuning of 6G-specialized models. Using 6G-Bench, we evaluate 22 foundation models spanning dense and mixture-of-experts architectures, short- and long-context designs (up to 1M tokens), and both open-weight and proprietary systems. Across models, deterministic single-shot accuracy (pass@1) spans a wide range from 0.22 to 0.82, highlighting substantial variation in semantic reasoning capability. Leading models achieve intent and policy reasoning accuracy in the range 0.87--0.89, while selective robustness analysis on reasoning-intensive tasks shows pass@5 values ranging from 0.20 to 0.91. To support open science and reproducibility, we release the 6G-Bench dataset on GitHub: https://github.com/maferrag/6G-Bench
out_of_scope.why_now: AI-native telecom is becoming a real standards topic, and this gives that niche an open benchmark spanning many frontier models. It is timely for researchers testing whether foundation models can support network control and semantic communication tasks.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The contribution is still a telecom-specific benchmark, not a result that changes mainstream AI training, serving, or evaluation practice. The evidence is based on synthetic multiple-choice scenarios rather than live network behavior, so the broader systems takeaway is limited.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow telecom application
No direct memory/storage contribution
Benchmark-heavy with limited real-world validation
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Show that performance on 6G-Bench predicts real network-control outcomes or surfaces failures that transfer to broader agentic AI systems.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can today's best foundation models actually reason well enough to help run a 6G network, or do they fall apart once AI leaves the chatbot lane?

out_of_scope.title: Edge-Assisted Multi-Robot Visual-Inertial SLAM with Efficient Communication
out_of_scope.abstract: The integration of cloud computing and edge computing is an effective way to achieve global consistent and real-time multi-robot Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM). Cloud computing effectively solves the problem of limited computing, communication and storage capacity of terminal equipment. However, limited bandwidth and extremely long communication links between terminal devices and the cloud result in serious performance degradation of multi-robot SLAM systems. To reduce the computational cost of feature tracking and improve the real-time performance of the robot, a lightweight SLAM method of optical flow tracking based on pyramid IMU prediction is proposed. On this basis, a centralized multi-robot SLAM system based on a robot-edge-cloud layered architecture is proposed to realize real-time collaborative SLAM. It avoids the problems of limited on-board computing resources and low execution efficiency of single robot. In this framework, only the feature points and keyframe descriptors are transmitted and lossless encoding and compression are carried out to realize real-time remote information transmission with limited bandwidth resources. This design reduces the actual bandwidth occupied in the process of data transmission, and does not cause the loss of SLAM accuracy caused by data compression. Through experimental verification on the EuRoC dataset, compared with the current most advanced local feature compression method, our method can achieve lower data volume feature transmission, and compared with the current advanced centralized multi-robot SLAM scheme, it can achieve the same or better positioning accuracy under low computational load.
out_of_scope.why_now: Edge and cloud-assisted robotics is making communication efficiency more important, especially when multiple robots need shared perception in real time. This paper targets that bottleneck by reducing what gets transmitted while keeping SLAM accuracy intact.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The contribution is tightly scoped to multi-robot visual-inertial SLAM and does not clearly change how the broader AI field builds, trains, serves, or evaluates modern models. Its bandwidth story is adjacent to memory systems, not a core result about AI memory hierarchies, KV cache, paging, or offload.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow robotics application
Memory link is communication-adjacent rather than storage-core
Limited validation beyond standard SLAM benchmarks
Weak transfer to mainstream AI systems workflows
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the communication and edge-offload ideas transfer to broader AI perception or multimodal serving stacks, with stronger real-world deployment results, would raise it.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a team of robots build one shared map by sending only the right visual clues to the edge instead of flooding the network with raw sensor data?

out_of_scope.title: Fingerprinting Concepts in Data Streams with Supervised and Unsupervised Meta-Information
out_of_scope.abstract: Streaming sources of data are becoming more common as the ability to collect data in real-time grows. A major concern in dealing with data streams is concept drift, a change in the distribution of data over time, for example, due to changes in environmental conditions. Representing concepts (stationary periods featuring similar behaviour) is a key idea in adapting to concept drift. By testing the similarity of a concept representation to a window of observations, we can detect concept drift to a new or previously seen recurring concept. Concept representations are constructed using meta-information features, values describing aspects of concept behaviour. We find that previously proposed concept representations rely on small numbers of meta-information features. These representations often cannot distinguish concepts, leaving systems vulnerable to concept drift. We propose FiCSUM, a general framework to represent both supervised and unsupervised behaviours of a concept in a fingerprint, a vector of many distinct meta-information features able to uniquely identify more concepts. Our dynamic weighting strategy learns which meta-information features describe concept drift in a given dataset, allowing a diverse set of meta-information features to be used at once. FiCSUM outperforms state-of-the-art methods over a range of 11 real world and synthetic datasets in both accuracy and modeling underlying concept drift.
out_of_scope.why_now: Streaming data and distribution shift are still practical problems, and this paper pushes concept-drift detection by building richer fingerprints of recurring concepts. The supervised plus unsupervised meta-feature framing is timely for anyone maintaining online models under changing conditions.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This does not clearly change how people build, train, serve, or evaluate modern frontier AI systems. The evidence is on data-stream benchmarks rather than contemporary large-model workloads, infrastructure, or hardware-constrained settings.
downgrade_reasons: No direct memory or storage mechanism
Weak connection to modern LLM or systems practice
Benchmarks are not realistic frontier-model workloads
Episode would skew toward niche stream-mining methods
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Show the method improving live adaptation in modern production AI systems such as LLM agents, recommendation, or multimodal streaming pipelines, ideally with a concrete systems or caching implication.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can a model fingerprint a live data stream well enough to notice when the world has changed before its predictions quietly go stale?
